questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timelines associated with CDP requests. A timely request (generally within 30 days of the CDP notice) is crucial for triggering the collection suspension. Missing the deadline can significantly limit your client's options.
questions.memoryAid: CDP = Collection Delaying Process (remember that timely CDP requests delay collection).

questions.question: An "equivalent hearing" differs from a CDP hearing in that:
options: It does not suspend collection and the determination is not appealable to Tax Court
It is conducted by the IRS Chief Counsel
It has the same rights as CDP
It requires full payment of tax
questions.explanation: An equivalent hearing (requested after 30 days but within 1 year) does not suspend collection action and the determination cannot be appealed to Tax Court, unlike a regular CDP hearing.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - An equivalent hearing is a hearing granted by the IRS when a taxpayer misses the deadline for requesting a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing. Unlike a CDP hearing, an equivalent hearing does *not* suspend collection actions, and the IRS determination is *not* appealable to the Tax Court. This is the key difference between the two types of hearings.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Equivalent hearings, like CDP hearings, are conducted by the IRS Independent Office of Appeals, not the IRS Chief Counsel. The Chief Counsel's office provides legal advice to the IRS, but does not conduct these types of hearings.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While an equivalent hearing provides the taxpayer an opportunity to discuss collection alternatives with the IRS, it does *not* provide the same rights as a CDP hearing. Specifically, collection is not suspended and the determination is not appealable to Tax Court.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Neither a CDP hearing nor an equivalent hearing requires full payment of the tax liability as a prerequisite for the hearing. The purpose of these hearings is to explore collection alternatives, such as installment agreements, offers in compromise, or innocent spouse relief, without requiring full payment upfront.

questions.educational: Collection Due Process (CDP) hearings and equivalent hearings are designed to protect taxpayers' rights during the collection process. CDP hearings, triggered by a notice of intent to levy or a notice of federal tax lien, offer more robust protections, including suspension of collection and appeal rights to Tax Court. Equivalent hearings provide a similar opportunity for discussion but without the same legal safeguards, reflecting the taxpayer's failure to meet the initial CDP deadline. Understanding the differences is crucial for advising clients on their options when facing IRS collection actions.
questions.examTip: Remember that the key difference between CDP and equivalent hearings for the EA exam is the suspension of collection and the right to appeal to Tax Court. If a client missed the CDP deadline, immediately explain the limitations of an equivalent hearing.
questions.memoryAid: CDP = Court & Pause (appeal to Tax Court and pause in collection); Equivalent = Neither.

questions.question: The Taxpayer Bill of Rights (TBOR) codified in IRC §7803(a)(3) provides ten fundamental rights. Which of the following accurately describes one of these rights?
options: The right to finality — knowing the maximum time to challenge an IRS position and the maximum time the IRS has to audit a tax year
The right to unlimited filing extensions without justification
The right to interest-free installment agreements regardless of balance owed
The right to refuse all IRS document requests during an examination
questions.explanation: The TBOR includes 10 rights, one being the right to finality — taxpayers can know the timeframes for IRS audits and for challenging IRS positions. Other rights include the right to be informed, quality service, pay no more than the correct tax, challenge an IRS position, appeal, privacy, confidentiality, retain representation, and a fair tax system.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The right to finality, as part of the Taxpayer Bill of Rights, ensures taxpayers understand the timeframe within which the IRS can audit a tax year and the period during which they can challenge an IRS position. This provides certainty and allows taxpayers to plan their financial affairs accordingly, aligning with the principles of fairness and transparency.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The Taxpayer Bill of Rights does not grant taxpayers the right to unlimited filing extensions without justification. Extensions are granted under specific circumstances and require a valid reason, as outlined in the tax code and IRS regulations. The standard extension is six months using Form 4868, but this is not unlimited or without requirement.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The Taxpayer Bill of Rights does not guarantee interest-free installment agreements regardless of the balance owed. Installment agreements are subject to IRS approval and typically involve accruing interest and penalties until the balance is paid. Interest-free agreements are extremely rare and only apply in very specific, limited circumstances.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The Taxpayer Bill of Rights does not grant taxpayers the right to refuse all IRS document requests during an examination. Taxpayers have a duty to cooperate with reasonable IRS requests for information and documentation. While taxpayers have the right to challenge the scope of a request, outright refusal is generally not permitted and can lead to penalties or other adverse actions.

questions.educational: The Taxpayer Bill of Rights (TBOR) aims to ensure fair and equitable treatment of taxpayers by the IRS. It's codified in IRC §7803(a)(3) and provides ten fundamental rights, including the right to be informed, the right to challenge the IRS's position, and the right to privacy. Understanding these rights is crucial for EAs to effectively represent their clients and advocate for their best interests during interactions with the IRS, especially during audits and appeals.
questions.examTip: As an EA, be prepared to advise clients on their rights under the TBOR, particularly the statute of limitations for audits and assessments, as this is a frequently tested topic on the SEE exam and a common issue in practice.
questions.memoryAid: FINER: Finality, Information, No more than correct tax, Equitable treatment, Representation (right to retain).

questions.question: The Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) can assist when:
options: A taxpayer is experiencing a significant hardship or is not receiving timely response from IRS
questions.explanation: TAS helps taxpayers experiencing significant hardship, facing imminent adverse action, or not receiving timely resolution of issues through normal IRS channels. Contact via Form 911.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) does not assist every taxpayer. TAS assistance is reserved for specific situations involving hardship or IRS unresponsiveness, not general requests.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - TAS assistance is not limited to criminal investigations. While TAS can assist taxpayers facing potential criminal tax issues, their primary focus is on resolving issues causing hardship or delayed resolution through normal IRS channels.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) is designed to help taxpayers experiencing significant hardship, facing an imminent adverse action (like a levy), or encountering prolonged delays or unresponsiveness from the IRS. This ensures fairness and efficient resolution of tax issues, as outlined in IRC § 7803(c).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - TAS assistance is not limited to wealthy taxpayers. TAS is available to all taxpayers, regardless of income level, who meet the criteria of experiencing hardship or facing unresolved issues with the IRS.

questions.educational: The Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) operates as an independent organization within the IRS to ensure taxpayers are treated fairly and know their rights. TAS helps resolve tax problems that taxpayers have been unable to resolve through normal IRS channels. Understanding TAS is crucial for an Enrolled Agent, as you'll need to recognize situations where your clients can benefit from their intervention, ensuring you fulfill your ethical duty to provide competent representation.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Form 911, Application for Taxpayer Advocate Assistance, is the key form for requesting TAS assistance for your clients experiencing hardship or IRS delays.
questions.memoryAid: TAS = Trouble? Advocate Service!

questions.question: Under IRC §7491, the burden of proof shifts to the IRS if the taxpayer:
options: Introduces credible evidence and meets substantiation and cooperation requirements
questions.explanation: Under §7491, burden of proof shifts to IRS if taxpayer produces credible evidence, maintains required records, substantiates items, and cooperates with reasonable IRS requests.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing a return is a standard requirement for all taxpayers, not a condition for shifting the burden of proof to the IRS under IRC §7491. While filing a return is necessary to trigger an audit, it doesn't automatically shift the burden of proof.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Waiving appeal rights might expedite the resolution of a tax dispute, but it does not shift the burden of proof to the IRS. The burden of proof shift is contingent on meeting specific requirements related to credible evidence and cooperation, not procedural choices like waiving appeal rights.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §7491 explicitly states that the burden of proof shifts to the IRS if the taxpayer introduces credible evidence with respect to any factual issue relevant to ascertaining the liability of the taxpayer, maintains required records, substantiates items pursuant to the Code, and cooperates with reasonable requests by the IRS for witnesses, information, documents, meetings, and interviews. Meeting all these conditions is crucial for the shift to occur.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Paying all tax due, while demonstrating good faith, doesn't automatically shift the burden of proof to the IRS under IRC §7491. The burden of proof shift is specifically tied to providing credible evidence, substantiating items, and cooperating with the IRS, regardless of whether the tax liability has been paid.

questions.educational: IRC §7491 aims to balance the power between taxpayers and the IRS. It recognizes that taxpayers often lack the resources to definitively prove their tax positions. By shifting the burden of proof under specific circumstances, it encourages the IRS to thoroughly investigate and substantiate its claims, ensuring fairness in tax administration, a key element of Circular 230's ethical responsibilities.
questions.examTip: Remember the acronym 'CSI' - Credible evidence, Substantiation, and IRS cooperation - as the key elements for shifting the burden of proof to the IRS under IRC §7491. As an EA, you must advise your clients on the importance of maintaining thorough records and cooperating with the IRS to potentially benefit from this provision.
questions.memoryAid: CSI: Credible evidence, Substantiation, IRS cooperation = Burden shifts to IRS.

questions.question: A taxpayer has the right to make an audio recording of an IRS interview if:
options: The taxpayer provides 10 days advance notice
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7521, taxpayers may record in-person interviews with 10 days advance written notice. The IRS may also record if they provide 10 days notice and provide a copy.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §7521(a)(1) explicitly grants taxpayers the right to make an audio recording of any in-person interview with the IRS, provided they give the IRS advance notice. The required notice period is 10 days before the interview.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Recording is not prohibited; in fact, taxpayers have a statutory right to record interviews under specific conditions outlined in IRC §7521(a)(1).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The right to record an IRS interview is not limited to Appeals; it applies to any in-person interview, regardless of the IRS function involved, as long as the 10-day notice requirement is met.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Taxpayers do not need IRS permission to record; they only need to provide the IRS with 10 days advance written notice as per IRC §7521(a)(1).

questions.educational: IRC §7521 aims to protect taxpayer rights during IRS interviews. By allowing taxpayers to record these interactions, the law promotes transparency and accountability, ensuring a fair process. This provision is important because it helps to level the playing field between the taxpayer and the IRS, and it can be crucial in resolving disputes later on.
questions.examTip: Remember that both the taxpayer and the IRS can record interviews, but both are subject to the 10-day advance notice requirement. This is a frequently tested area of taxpayer rights on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: T-APE: Taxpayer Audio Permission = 10 days advance notice.

questions.question: During an IRS interview, a taxpayer has the right to:
options: Suspend the interview to consult with a representative
Demand the interview be conducted at their home
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7521(b)(2), if at any point during an interview the taxpayer clearly requests to consult with a representative, the interview must be suspended.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §7521(b)(2) explicitly grants taxpayers the right to suspend an IRS interview to consult with a representative. This ensures taxpayers have adequate support and understanding during potentially complex or adversarial interactions with the IRS.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While taxpayers have the right to remain silent to avoid self-incrimination under the Fifth Amendment, they cannot simply refuse all questions during an IRS interview without potential consequences. Refusal to answer relevant questions can lead to the IRS issuing a summons or taking other enforcement actions.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While taxpayers generally have the right to record their interactions with the IRS, they must provide prior notice to the IRS. Recording without notice is generally not permitted and could have legal ramifications.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS generally determines the location of an interview, typically at an IRS office. While taxpayers can request a different location, including their home, the IRS is not obligated to grant this request. The IRS needs to balance taxpayer convenience with operational efficiency and security.

questions.educational: Taxpayer rights are a cornerstone of the tax system, designed to ensure fairness and protect individuals from potential abuse of power by the IRS. Understanding these rights, including the right to representation and the right to suspend an interview, is crucial for Enrolled Agents to effectively advocate for their clients and navigate IRS procedures. These rights are codified in the Internal Revenue Code and Circular 230, emphasizing the importance of ethical and competent representation.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that taxpayer rights are often tested in scenarios involving IRS interactions. Focus on the specific rights outlined in IRC §7521 and Circular 230 to identify the correct course of action for representing your client.
questions.memoryAid: R.A.S. - Right to suspend, to consult with a Representative, during an IRS interview.

questions.question: IRS employees are prohibited from:
options: Using their positions to harass or intimidate taxpayers
questions.explanation: IRC §7804(b) prohibits IRS employees from using their authority to harass or intimidate taxpayers. Violations can result in disciplinary action and taxpayers can report misconduct.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRS employees routinely contact taxpayers for various reasons, including audits, notices, and correspondence. Prohibiting all contact would make tax administration impossible.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7804(b) explicitly prohibits IRS employees from using their official positions to harass or intimidate taxpayers. This protection is a fundamental taxpayer right and a cornerstone of fair tax administration. Such behavior is a serious breach of ethics and can result in severe penalties for the employee.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Conducting audits is a core function of the IRS. While there are rules and procedures governing how audits are conducted, IRS employees are certainly not prohibited from performing them.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Issuing notices is a routine part of IRS operations. Notices are used to inform taxpayers of assessments, refunds, and other important information. Prohibiting notices would cripple the IRS's ability to communicate with taxpayers.

questions.educational: Taxpayer rights are paramount in the U.S. tax system. The prohibition against harassment and intimidation by IRS employees is designed to ensure fair and impartial tax administration. Understanding these rights, as outlined in publications like Publication 1, is crucial for Enrolled Agents to effectively represent their clients and advocate for their interests before the IRS.
questions.examTip: Remember that Enrolled Agents have a duty to report any instances of IRS misconduct or abuse of power they observe. Familiarize yourself with the procedures for reporting such incidents.
questions.memoryAid: H.I.T. - Harassment, Intimidation, Threatening behavior by IRS employees is a big NO-NO.

questions.question: The IRS must accept a guaranteed installment agreement if the taxpayer:
options: Owes $10,000 or less (excluding penalties/interest), can pay within 3 years, and meets filing/payment compliance requirements
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6159(c), the IRS must accept an installment agreement if the liability is $10,000 or less (excluding P&I), payable within 3 years, and the taxpayer has filed all returns and not had an IA in prior 5 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS is not required to accept a guaranteed installment agreement simply because a taxpayer owes any amount. Specific criteria related to the amount owed, repayment timeframe, and compliance history must be met.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6159(c) mandates the IRS to accept a guaranteed installment agreement if the tax liability is $10,000 or less (excluding penalties and interest), can be paid off within three years, and the taxpayer meets specific filing and payment compliance requirements. These requirements include having filed all required returns and not having defaulted on a prior installment agreement within the past five years.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Being a first-time taxpayer is not a criterion for a guaranteed installment agreement. While the IRS may be more lenient with first-time taxpayers in certain situations, a guaranteed installment agreement is based on the amount owed, repayment timeframe, and compliance history, not taxpayer status.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the IRS has discretion to enter into installment agreements for liabilities up to $50,000 or more, a *guaranteed* installment agreement is specifically limited to liabilities of $10,000 or less (excluding penalties and interest) under IRC §6159(c).

questions.educational: The guaranteed installment agreement provisions in IRC §6159(c) aim to provide a streamlined process for taxpayers with relatively small tax liabilities to resolve their debts. This helps reduce administrative burden for both the IRS and taxpayers, encouraging prompt payment and compliance. Understanding the specific requirements for guaranteed installment agreements is crucial for EAs advising clients on tax resolution options.
questions.examTip: Remember the $10,000 limit and the 3-year repayment period for guaranteed installment agreements. The exam often tests the specific dollar thresholds and timeframes associated with various IRS procedures.
questions.memoryAid: Think '10-3 Guarantee': $10,000 or less, 3 years to pay, guaranteed acceptance (assuming other criteria are met).

questions.question: A streamlined installment agreement is available for individuals who owe:
options: $50,000 or less in combined balance and can pay within 72 months or CSED
Any amount if they can pay within 5 years
questions.explanation: Streamlined IAs are available for balances of $50,000 or less (now $50K for individuals using direct debit) payable within 72 months or before CSED, whichever is shorter. No financial statement required.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A streamlined installment agreement is indeed available to individual taxpayers who owe $50,000 or less in combined tax, penalties, and interest. This amount can be paid off within 72 months or before the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED), whichever is earlier, and typically doesn't require a detailed financial statement.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the ability to pay within 5 years is a factor in installment agreements, it's not the sole criterion for a *streamlined* installment agreement. Streamlined agreements have a debt limit ($50,000) that this option ignores.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The $10,000 limit is too restrictive. Streamlined installment agreements are available for balances up to $50,000, making this option significantly understated.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While $100,000 is a possible balance for an installment agreement, it exceeds the $50,000 limit for a *streamlined* installment agreement. Balances over $50,000 require a more detailed application process and financial review.

questions.educational: Installment agreements allow taxpayers to pay their tax liabilities over time. The IRS offers streamlined installment agreements to simplify the process for taxpayers with smaller balances, reducing the administrative burden for both the taxpayer and the IRS. Understanding the criteria for streamlined agreements is crucial for advising clients on the most efficient way to resolve their tax debts and avoid penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific terminology used in the question. The word 'streamlined' is a key indicator that the $50,000 limit applies. Also, remember the 72-month or CSED limitation.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Streamlined Fifty' - Streamlined IAs up to $50,000.

questions.question: Form 433-A is used for:
options: Collection information statement for wage earners and self-employed
questions.explanation: Form 433-A (Collection Information Statement for Wage Earners and Self-Employed Individuals) provides detailed financial information for installment agreements above streamlined thresholds or OIC applications.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Form 433-A, titled "Collection Information Statement for Wage Earners and Self-Employed Individuals," is used by the IRS to gather detailed financial information from taxpayers who are seeking to resolve their tax liabilities through installment agreements or other collection alternatives. This form helps the IRS assess the taxpayer's ability to pay and determine an appropriate payment plan.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 656 is the application form used to submit an Offer in Compromise (OIC) to the IRS. While Form 433-A may be *included* as part of an OIC submission to provide detailed financial information, it is not the OIC application itself.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 4868 is used to request an automatic extension of time to file a U.S. Individual Income Tax Return (Form 1040). It does not relate to collection information or installment agreements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative, is used to authorize an individual to represent a taxpayer before the IRS. It grants the representative the authority to perform certain actions on the taxpayer's behalf, but it does not gather financial information for collection purposes.

questions.educational: The IRS uses various forms to collect information and manage tax liabilities. Form 433-A is crucial in the collection process because it allows the IRS to understand a taxpayer's financial situation, including income, expenses, assets, and liabilities. This information is essential for determining the taxpayer's ability to pay and negotiating appropriate payment arrangements, such as installment agreements, which are governed by IRC Section 6159.
questions.examTip: Remember the purpose of Form 433-A: it's all about the IRS getting a clear picture of the taxpayer's finances to determine how they can pay their tax debt. On the EA exam, pay close attention to the context of the question – is it about collection, representation, or filing?
questions.memoryAid: 433-A: 'A' stands for 'Assets' - think of it as the form to assess a taxpayer's assets and ability to pay.

questions.question: The setup fee for an installment agreement:
options: Varies based on whether it is direct debit, low-income, and online vs. paper application
questions.explanation: Setup fees vary: online applications cost less than phone/mail; direct debit agreements cost less than regular; low-income taxpayers may qualify for reduced or waived fees.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The installment agreement setup fee is not a percentage of the balance owed. It is a fixed fee that varies based on specific criteria.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS charges a setup fee for installment agreements, and the amount varies depending on how the agreement is established and the taxpayer's circumstances. Direct debit agreements and online applications generally have lower fees, and low-income taxpayers may qualify for reduced or waived fees, as detailed in IRS guidance.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS does not waive the setup fee for all taxpayers. While low-income taxpayers may qualify for a waiver or reduced fee, most taxpayers are required to pay a setup fee for installment agreements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The installment agreement setup fee is not a flat $500. The fee varies based on factors such as the method of payment (direct debit vs. other methods), how the agreement is applied for (online vs. paper), and the taxpayer's income level.

questions.educational: Installment agreements allow taxpayers to pay their tax liabilities over time. The IRS charges a setup fee to cover the administrative costs associated with establishing and managing these agreements. Encouraging direct debit and online applications reduces IRS processing costs, hence the lower fees. Understanding the nuances of installment agreements is crucial for EAs to advise clients on the most cost-effective payment options and navigate IRS procedures effectively.
questions.examTip: Remember the varying installment agreement fees based on payment method and income status; this is a frequently tested area on the EA exam, especially regarding advising clients on minimizing costs.
questions.memoryAid: D-O-L: Direct debit, Online, Low-income = Lower fees for installment agreements.

questions.question: Form 9465 is used to:
questions.explanation: Form 9465 (Installment Agreement Request) is used to request a monthly payment plan for taxes owed. It can be filed with a return or separately.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 4868, Application for Automatic Extension of Time to File U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, is used to request an extension of time to file, not Form 9465.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 8379, Injured Spouse Allocation, is used to claim injured spouse relief, which allows a spouse to recover their share of a refund that was applied to the other spouse's past-due obligations. Form 9465 is unrelated to this process.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Form 9465, Installment Agreement Request, is the form taxpayers use to request a monthly payment plan (installment agreement) for unpaid taxes, penalties, and interest. The IRS considers various factors when evaluating the request, including the taxpayer's ability to pay and prior compliance history.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Taxpayers generally use Form 843, Claim for Refund and Request for Abatement, or petition the Tax Court to appeal an audit determination. Form 9465 is specifically for requesting installment agreements.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a critical tool for taxpayers who cannot afford to pay their tax liabilities in full. The IRS offers various types of installment agreements, including streamlined agreements for smaller balances and agreements requiring financial statements for larger balances. Understanding the requirements and procedures for obtaining an installment agreement is essential for representing clients before the IRS, as it can help them avoid penalties and collection actions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific forms used for different IRS procedures. Knowing which form to use in a given situation is a frequently tested topic.
questions.memoryAid: 9465? Think 'Pay 4 Taxes, Get Set' (installment agreement).

questions.question: A Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA) allows:
options: Payment of full balance in extended time
Monthly payments that will not fully pay the liability before the collection statute expires
questions.explanation: A PPIA is for taxpayers who cannot pay in full before the CSED expires. The IRS accepts payments based on ability to pay, and the remaining balance is written off when CSED expires.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA) does not waive all penalties. Penalties continue to accrue until the tax liability is fully paid or the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) is reached.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A PPIA is specifically for situations where the taxpayer cannot pay the full balance within the statutory collection period. If the taxpayer could pay in full within the collection period, a standard installment agreement would be used.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A PPIA is designed for taxpayers who can make monthly payments, but those payments will not fully satisfy the tax liability before the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). The IRS accepts these payments based on the taxpayer's ability to pay, and any remaining balance is generally written off when the CSED is reached.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a PPIA involves making payments, it is not a one-time partial payment. It is an agreement for ongoing monthly payments based on the taxpayer's ability to pay over a period of time.

questions.educational: Installment agreements, including PPIAs, are governed by IRC Section 6159. The IRS grants installment agreements to facilitate tax collection when taxpayers cannot immediately pay their full tax liability. PPIAs are a specific type of installment agreement used when the taxpayer's ability to pay is less than the total liability within the collection statute. Understanding the different types of installment agreements and their requirements is crucial for effective taxpayer representation.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the taxpayer's ability to pay and the remaining time on the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) when determining the appropriate type of installment agreement. A PPIA is only appropriate when full payment is impossible before the CSED.
questions.memoryAid: PPIA: Partial Payment, Possible Inability to Afford (full payment).

questions.question: The IRS may terminate an installment agreement if the taxpayer:
options: Fails to pay an installment, incurs new liability, fails to file, or provides inaccurate financial information
questions.explanation: IRS may terminate an IA for: default on payment, new tax liability, failure to file returns, inaccurate/incomplete financial disclosure, or changed financial condition. 30-day notice required before termination.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The IRS can terminate an installment agreement if the taxpayer defaults on payments, incurs a new tax liability, fails to file required tax returns, or provides inaccurate or incomplete information. These actions demonstrate a failure to comply with the terms of the agreement or a change in the taxpayer's ability to meet their tax obligations, as outlined in IRS guidance.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Making all payments on time is the desired outcome of an installment agreement and would not be grounds for termination. In fact, consistent on-time payments demonstrate the taxpayer's adherence to the agreement.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Moving to a new address, by itself, is not grounds for termination of an installment agreement. The taxpayer is generally required to notify the IRS of a change of address, but simply moving does not violate the terms of the agreement.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Requesting a modification of an installment agreement is not grounds for automatic termination. The IRS may consider the request and either approve, deny, or propose an alternative modification, but the agreement remains in effect unless terminated for other reasons.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a critical tool for taxpayers who cannot afford to pay their tax liabilities in full. The IRS grants these agreements with the understanding that the taxpayer will adhere to specific terms, including timely payments and continued compliance with tax laws. Termination provisions exist to protect the government's interest and ensure that taxpayers are genuinely committed to resolving their tax debt.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the conditions that trigger installment agreement termination, as the IRS must provide a 30-day notice before terminating the agreement, giving the taxpayer an opportunity to rectify the situation. Knowing these triggers is crucial for advising clients on maintaining their installment agreements.
questions.memoryAid: FINF: Failure to pay, Incur new liability, No filing, False information.

questions.question: When an installment agreement is in effect:
options: The IRS cannot file a federal tax lien
The IRS may still file a Notice of Federal Tax Lien for balances over $10,000
questions.explanation: An IA does not prevent lien filing. However, for streamlined IAs under $25,000 with direct debit, the IRS generally withdraws existing liens after the IA is established.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS retains the right to file a Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) even when an installment agreement is in effect. An installment agreement doesn't automatically prevent lien filing; it's a separate decision by the IRS.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS may still file a Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) even with an installment agreement, especially for balances exceeding $10,000. Filing a lien protects the government's interest in the taxpayer's property and ensures priority over other creditors. While the IRS may choose not to file a lien in certain circumstances (e.g., streamlined installment agreements under $25,000 with direct debit), it's not prohibited.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Interest continues to accrue on the unpaid balance even when an installment agreement is in place. An installment agreement allows taxpayers to make payments over time, but it doesn't stop the accrual of interest and penalties until the debt is fully satisfied.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An installment agreement does not automatically release existing liens. Liens are only released when the tax liability is fully paid, or the IRS determines that releasing the lien is in the best interest of the government and the taxpayer (e.g., it facilitates the taxpayer's ability to pay). In some cases, for streamlined installment agreements under $25,000 with direct debit, the IRS will withdraw existing liens after the IA is established, but this is not an automatic release.

questions.educational: Installment agreements allow taxpayers to satisfy their tax liabilities through a series of payments. However, the IRS retains certain rights to protect its interests, including the ability to file a Notice of Federal Tax Lien. This ensures the government's claim is secured against other creditors and encourages compliance. Understanding the interplay between installment agreements and lien filings is crucial for advising clients on the best course of action to resolve their tax debts.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the specific conditions of an installment agreement. While the IRS may withdraw a lien under certain streamlined agreements, it's not a guaranteed outcome, and interest always continues to accrue.
questions.memoryAid: IA doesn't stop NFTL (Installment Agreement doesn't stop Notice of Federal Tax Lien)

questions.question: During an installment agreement:
options: Interest and the failure-to-pay penalty continue to accrue on the unpaid balance
questions.explanation: Interest continues at the federal short-term rate plus 3%. The failure-to-pay penalty (0.5%/month) is reduced to 0.25%/month while an IA is in effect, but both continue accruing.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Interest and penalties do *not* stop accruing during an installment agreement. While the failure-to-pay penalty is reduced, both interest and penalties continue to be assessed on the outstanding balance.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An installment agreement allows taxpayers to pay their tax liability over time, but it doesn't eliminate the underlying obligation to pay interest and penalties. While the failure-to-pay penalty is reduced from 0.5% to 0.25% per month, both interest and the reduced penalty continue to accrue until the balance is paid in full, as per IRC Section 6601 and 6651.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Interest continues to accrue on the unpaid balance during an installment agreement, in addition to the reduced failure-to-pay penalty. This is because the taxpayer is still using the government's money and must compensate for the time value of money.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While interest does continue to accrue, the failure-to-pay penalty also continues to accrue, albeit at a reduced rate of 0.25% per month. Therefore, this option is incomplete.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a critical tool for taxpayers who cannot afford to pay their tax liability in full. The IRS allows these agreements to facilitate tax collection and avoid more drastic measures like levies and liens. However, the government still requires compensation for the delayed payment in the form of interest and a reduced failure-to-pay penalty, ensuring fairness to taxpayers who pay on time and incentivizing prompt payment.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that installment agreements provide relief but don't eliminate the obligation to pay interest and penalties. Be prepared to calculate the total amount due, considering the reduced penalty rate.
questions.memoryAid: IA = Interest Always (and Penalties, Albeit Reduced)

questions.question: The IRS Online Payment Agreement tool allows taxpayers to:
options: Set up, modify, or reinstate an installment agreement electronically
questions.explanation: The IRS Online Payment Agreement (OPA) tool at IRS.gov allows qualifying taxpayers to apply for IAs, modify existing agreements, or reinstate defaulted agreements online.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The IRS Online Payment Agreement (OPA) tool is specifically designed to allow taxpayers to establish, change, or revive installment agreements electronically. This streamlines the process and reduces the need for paper forms and correspondence.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While taxpayers *can* view their balance online through other IRS tools, the Online Payment Agreement tool's primary function extends beyond simply viewing the balance; it facilitates managing installment agreements.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The Online Payment Agreement tool is not designed for contesting liabilities. Taxpayers must use separate procedures, such as filing an amended return or requesting an audit reconsideration, to dispute their tax obligations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While taxpayers *can* make one-time payments through other IRS online portals, the Online Payment Agreement tool's focus is on setting up and managing installment agreements, which involve a series of payments over time, not just a single payment.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a crucial tool for taxpayers who cannot afford to pay their tax liability in full when it is due. The IRS provides the Online Payment Agreement tool to make it easier for taxpayers to manage their payment obligations and avoid further penalties and interest. Understanding the requirements and procedures for installment agreements is essential for Enrolled Agents, as they frequently assist clients in navigating these arrangements.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS prioritizes making it easy for taxpayers to pay their taxes. The Online Payment Agreement tool is a prime example of this, so be familiar with its capabilities and limitations.
questions.memoryAid: OPA: Online Payment Agreement = Organize Payment Arrangements

questions.question: An Offer in Compromise may be submitted based on:
options: Doubt as to collectibility, doubt as to liability, or effective tax administration
questions.explanation: OICs may be based on: (1) doubt as to liability (legitimate dispute about tax owed), (2) doubt as to collectibility (cannot pay full amount), or (3) effective tax administration (collection would cause hardship/unfair).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While taxpayers can *choose* to submit an Offer in Compromise (OIC), the IRS will only consider it based on specific, defined grounds. An OIC based on any reason the taxpayer chooses would be rejected.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An Offer in Compromise (OIC) can be submitted based on three grounds: doubt as to liability (a legitimate dispute exists regarding the amount of tax owed), doubt as to collectibility (the taxpayer cannot afford to pay the full amount), or effective tax administration (collection would create economic hardship or be detrimental to equity and public policy). These are the only acceptable bases for an OIC.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Financial hardship is a component of 'effective tax administration,' which is a valid reason for an OIC. However, 'doubt as to liability' and 'doubt as to collectibility' are also valid reasons, making this option incomplete and therefore incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 'Doubt as to collectibility' is a valid reason for an OIC. However, 'doubt as to liability' and 'effective tax administration' are also valid reasons, making this option incomplete and therefore incorrect.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program is a critical tool for resolving tax liabilities when taxpayers face significant financial challenges or have legitimate disputes with the IRS. The IRS carefully scrutinizes OICs to ensure fairness and consistency, protecting the government's interest while providing relief to taxpayers who genuinely qualify. Understanding the specific grounds for an OIC is essential for enrolled agents to effectively represent their clients and navigate the complexities of IRS procedures.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS has strict guidelines for OICs. Memorize the three acceptable bases: doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, and effective tax administration. Be prepared to differentiate between these grounds in scenario-based questions.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'DEC' for Doubt (as to liability), Effective tax administration, and Collectibility. These are the three reasons for an OIC.

questions.question: Form 656 is used to:
questions.explanation: Form 656 (Offer in Compromise) is the primary form for submitting an OIC based on doubt as to collectibility or effective tax administration. Form 656-L is used for doubt as to liability.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 656 is not used to file an appeal. Appeals are generally initiated by filing Form 9423, Collection Appeal Request, or by petitioning the Tax Court.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 656, Offer in Compromise, is the form taxpayers use to request an agreement with the IRS to settle their tax debt for a lower amount than what they owe. This is typically based on doubt as to collectibility (the IRS doubts it can collect the full amount) or doubt as to liability (the taxpayer disputes the amount owed), or effective tax administration (payment would cause economic hardship).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 656 is not used to request an extension to file a tax return. Extensions are requested using forms specific to the type of return, such as Form 4868 for individual income tax returns.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 656 is not used to request penalty abatement. Penalty abatement requests are typically made using Form 843, Claim for Refund and Request for Abatement, or by writing a letter to the IRS explaining the reasonable cause for the penalty.

questions.educational: Offers in Compromise (OICs) represent a significant area of tax resolution. The IRS will consider an OIC if the taxpayer demonstrates an inability to pay the full tax liability, doubt as to the accuracy of the assessment, or if exceptional circumstances exist where payment would cause economic hardship or be detrimental to public policy. Understanding the criteria for OICs and the proper use of Form 656 is crucial for representing clients effectively before the IRS.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS has strict guidelines for accepting OICs. Be prepared to advise clients on the required documentation and financial information needed to support their offer, and understand the implications of submitting an offer, such as the suspension of collection activity.
questions.memoryAid: 656: OIC - Offer In Compromise. Think '656 - Settle the debt quick!'

questions.question: The OIC application fee is:
options: $205, plus 20% of the offer amount (or first monthly payment for periodic offers)
questions.explanation: The OIC fee is $205 plus 20% of offer (lump sum) or first monthly payment (periodic). Low-income taxpayers (at/below 250% of poverty guidelines) are exempt from fee and initial payment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The correct fee structure for an Offer in Compromise (OIC) application includes a base fee of $205, plus 20% of the total offer amount if submitting a lump-sum offer, or 20% of the first monthly payment if submitting a periodic payment offer. This structure helps the IRS offset the costs associated with processing OICs while also ensuring taxpayers have some 'skin in the game'.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The OIC application fee is not calculated as a percentage of the total tax liability. The fee is a fixed amount plus a percentage of the offer amount, not the total tax owed.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While certain low-income taxpayers may qualify for a waiver of the OIC application fee, the fee is not waived for all taxpayers. Taxpayers must meet specific income guidelines (at or below 250% of the poverty guidelines) to be eligible for the waiver.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The OIC application fee is not a flat $500. It consists of a base fee of $205 plus a percentage of the offer amount or first periodic payment.

questions.educational: Offers in Compromise (OICs) are agreements between a taxpayer and the IRS that settle the taxpayer's tax liabilities for less than the full amount owed. The IRS considers various factors when evaluating an OIC, including the taxpayer's ability to pay, income, expenses, and asset equity. Understanding the OIC process, including the application fee and waiver criteria, is crucial for Enrolled Agents assisting clients with tax debt resolution.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the OIC application fee can be a distractor. Focus on the $205 base fee and the 20% calculation based on the offer amount or first payment, and be aware of the low-income waiver provision.
questions.memoryAid: OIC Fee: '$205 + 20%' - Think 'Two-Oh-Five, Plus Twenty Percent' to remember the base fee and percentage.

questions.question: The IRS determines an acceptable OIC amount based on:
options: What the taxpayer offers to pay
Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) - equity in assets plus future income
questions.explanation: RCP = equity in assets (quick sale value minus encumbrances) + future income (based on remaining CSED). The offer must generally equal or exceed RCP for acceptance.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Prior tax payments are considered when determining the total tax liability, but they do not directly determine the acceptable Offer in Compromise (OIC) amount. The OIC focuses on the taxpayer's current ability to pay.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the taxpayer's offer is the starting point for OIC negotiations, the IRS will not automatically accept whatever the taxpayer proposes. The IRS independently calculates the Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) and uses that as a baseline for acceptance.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The IRS determines an acceptable Offer in Compromise (OIC) amount based on the taxpayer's Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP). RCP includes the equity in the taxpayer's assets (quick sale value less any encumbrances) and their future income potential (based on their remaining time on the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED)). The IRS generally requires the offer to equal or exceed the RCP.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the total liability is a factor in considering an OIC, the IRS does not simply accept a fixed percentage of the total liability. The RCP calculation, which considers assets and future income, is the primary determinant.

questions.educational: Offers in Compromise (OICs) allow taxpayers to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. The IRS grants OICs when the taxpayer cannot pay the full amount, there is doubt as to the liability, or to promote effective tax administration. Understanding the components of RCP is crucial, as it directly impacts the likelihood of an OIC being accepted and is a key area tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: When calculating RCP for an OIC, remember to use the quick sale value of assets, not the fair market value, and to consider the remaining time on the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) when projecting future income.
questions.memoryAid: OIC = RCP (Offer In Compromise = Reasonable Collection Potential)

questions.question: OIC payment options include:
options: Lump sum (paid within 5 months) or periodic payment (paid within 24 months)
Any payment over any time period
questions.explanation: Lump Sum Cash offers require 20% with application and balance within 5 months of acceptance. Periodic Payment offers require first payment with application and remaining payments within 6-24 months.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Offers in Compromise (OICs) are not limited to lump-sum payments only. The IRS provides options for both lump-sum and periodic payments to accommodate taxpayers' varying financial situations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - OICs are not limited to periodic payments only. Taxpayers can also choose to submit a lump-sum offer, which may be more appealing to the IRS in certain circumstances.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - This option accurately reflects the two main payment structures for OICs. Lump Sum Cash offers require the balance to be paid within 5 months of acceptance, while Periodic Payment offers require payments to be completed within 6 to 24 months. The IRS considers the payment timeframe when evaluating an OIC.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the IRS aims to work with taxpayers, OIC payment options are not open to any payment over any time period. The IRS sets specific timeframes for lump-sum and periodic payments to ensure timely resolution of tax liabilities.

questions.educational: Offers in Compromise (OICs) allow taxpayers to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. The IRS considers various factors when evaluating an OIC, including the taxpayer's ability to pay, income, expenses, and asset equity. The payment options (lump sum or periodic) are a key component of the offer and influence the IRS's decision to accept or reject it. Understanding the OIC process is crucial for enrolled agents advising clients facing significant tax debt.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the specific timeframes for OIC payment options: 5 months for lump sum and 6-24 months for periodic payments. Knowing these timelines is essential for correctly answering questions about OIC eligibility and procedures.
questions.memoryAid: OIC: '5 for Fast, 24 for Later' (5 months for lump sum, up to 24 months for periodic)

questions.question: After an OIC is accepted, the taxpayer must remain in filing and payment compliance for:
questions.explanation: Accepted OIC terms require 5 years of tax compliance (file all returns, pay all taxes). Noncompliance during this period can reinstate the original liability minus payments made.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The compliance period for an accepted Offer in Compromise (OIC) is not 3 years. The IRS requires a longer period of compliance to ensure the taxpayer is committed to fulfilling their tax obligations going forward.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An accepted Offer in Compromise (OIC) requires a period of compliance. The IRS requires taxpayers to remain compliant for a set period after acceptance to ensure they can maintain their tax obligations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An accepted Offer in Compromise (OIC) requires the taxpayer to remain compliant with all filing and payment requirements for five years from the date the offer is accepted. This means filing all required tax returns on time and paying all taxes due on time. Failure to comply can result in the reinstatement of the original tax liability.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the IRS takes compliance seriously, the compliance period for an accepted Offer in Compromise (OIC) is not 10 years. The required compliance period is 5 years.

questions.educational: The 5-year compliance period following an accepted Offer in Compromise (OIC) is designed to ensure taxpayers demonstrate a sustained commitment to meeting their tax obligations. This requirement helps protect the government's interest by mitigating the risk of future noncompliance and reinforcing the integrity of the tax system. It connects to the broader exam topics of IRS collection procedures and taxpayer rights.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 5-year compliance period for OICs starts from the date of acceptance, not the date of the offer or the date of the agreement.
questions.memoryAid: OIC = 'Oh I Comply' for 5 years!

questions.question: During the OIC consideration period and the year it is accepted:
options: The IRS will apply refunds to the tax liability covered by the OIC
questions.explanation: Any refunds due during OIC consideration and for the tax year of acceptance are applied to the tax liability. Taxpayers should adjust withholding to avoid generating refunds.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - During the OIC consideration period and the year it is accepted, refunds are not issued normally. The IRS has the right to offset any refunds against the tax liability that is the subject of the OIC.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - According to IRS policy, any refunds due to the taxpayer during the period an Offer in Compromise (OIC) is being considered, and for the tax year the OIC is accepted, will be applied to the underlying tax liability. This is to ensure the IRS recovers as much of the debt as possible.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - There is no provision for refunds to be doubled during an OIC consideration period or after acceptance. This is a distractor option.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Refunds are not placed in escrow during the OIC consideration period. Instead, the IRS directly applies the refund to the outstanding tax liability covered by the OIC.

questions.educational: The IRS's policy of applying refunds during the OIC process is rooted in its duty to collect outstanding tax liabilities efficiently. By offsetting refunds, the IRS reduces the overall debt and minimizes the risk of non-payment. This aligns with the broader goal of ensuring tax compliance and fairness in the tax system, a key focus of the Enrolled Agent's role.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS will seize refunds during OIC consideration and for the year of acceptance. Advise your clients to adjust their withholding to minimize potential refunds during this period to avoid disappointment.
questions.memoryAid: OIC = Offset Incoming Cash (refunds)

questions.question: If an OIC is rejected, the taxpayer may:
options: Request an appeal to the IRS Office of Appeals within 30 days
questions.explanation: Taxpayers may appeal an OIC rejection to the IRS Office of Appeals within 30 days of the rejection letter. This is an administrative appeal, not a court action.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Taxpayers have the right to appeal an Offer in Compromise (OIC) rejection to the IRS Office of Appeals. This is a crucial taxpayer right afforded by administrative procedures, allowing for a review of the initial determination before further legal action is considered, and the taxpayer must request the appeal within 30 days of the rejection letter.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While Tax Court is an avenue for resolving tax disputes, it's not the immediate next step after an OIC rejection. Taxpayers generally must exhaust their administrative remedies, such as appealing to the IRS Office of Appeals, before petitioning Tax Court.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS provides avenues for taxpayers to challenge adverse decisions, including OIC rejections. To say a rejection is final without any recourse is incorrect, as it violates the taxpayer's right to appeal within the IRS administrative framework.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Suing in District Court is a legal option for tax disputes, but it's not the immediate next step after an OIC rejection. Taxpayers must generally exhaust their administrative remedies, including appealing to the IRS Office of Appeals, before pursuing litigation in District Court.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program allows taxpayers facing financial hardship to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. The IRS reviews OICs based on factors like ability to pay, income, expenses, and asset equity. If an OIC is rejected, the taxpayer has the right to appeal this decision within the IRS administrative framework, ensuring fairness and due process.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the specific timelines for appealing IRS decisions, especially the 30-day window for appealing an OIC rejection to the IRS Office of Appeals; this is a frequently tested area.
questions.memoryAid: OIC Rejection? 30 Days to Rejection!

questions.question: During OIC consideration:
options: The collection statute is suspended plus 30 additional days
questions.explanation: The 10-year Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) is suspended while an OIC is pending, plus 30 days after rejection or withdrawal. This prevents using OICs to run out the clock.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) is suspended while an Offer in Compromise (OIC) is pending, plus an additional 30 days after the OIC is rejected or withdrawn. This prevents taxpayers from strategically using the OIC process to delay collection until the CSED expires, effectively avoiding the tax liability.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While interest continues to accrue during OIC consideration, the collection statute is also suspended. This option only addresses one aspect of the impact on the CSED and is therefore incomplete and incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The CSED does *not* continue to run during OIC consideration. The CSED is suspended, meaning the countdown towards the expiration of the collection statute is paused. This is a critical distinction.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The statute does *not* expire immediately upon submission of an OIC. Instead, the collection statute is suspended, meaning it's paused, not immediately terminated.

questions.educational: The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) is a 10-year period in which the IRS can legally collect a tax debt. Offers in Compromise (OICs) are agreements between a taxpayer and the IRS that resolve the taxpayer's tax liability for a lower amount than what is originally owed. Suspending the CSED during OIC consideration ensures the IRS has adequate time to evaluate the offer and pursue collection if the offer is rejected, preventing taxpayers from exploiting the OIC process to avoid paying their taxes.
questions.examTip: Remember that the CSED suspension applies from the date the IRS accepts the OIC for processing until 30 days after the OIC is rejected or withdrawn. This is a common point of confusion on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: OIC = Offer In Collection suspension (think: the offer *suspends* collection activity)

questions.question: If the IRS does not make a determination on an OIC within 24 months:
options: The OIC is transferred to Appeals
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7122(f), if the IRS fails to make a determination within 24 months, the OIC is deemed accepted. The 24 months excludes any periods the OIC is returned to taxpayer.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS's failure to act within 24 months results in acceptance, not rejection, of the OIC as per IRC §7122(f).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7122(f) explicitly states that if the IRS does not make a determination on an Offer in Compromise (OIC) within 24 months of the date the IRS received the offer, the OIC is deemed accepted. This provision encourages the IRS to process OICs in a timely manner.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While Appeals is involved in the OIC process in certain situations, the inaction of the IRS within 24 months leads to acceptance, not an automatic transfer to Appeals.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The taxpayer does not need to resubmit the OIC if the IRS fails to make a determination within 24 months; instead, the OIC is deemed accepted according to IRC §7122(f).

questions.educational: The 24-month rule for OIC determinations serves as a safeguard for taxpayers, preventing the IRS from indefinitely delaying a decision. This rule balances the IRS's need to thoroughly investigate offers with the taxpayer's right to a timely resolution. Understanding OIC procedures is crucial for Enrolled Agents, as it directly impacts their ability to effectively represent clients facing tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 24-month period for OIC determinations excludes any time the OIC is returned to the taxpayer for additional information or clarification. Be prepared to calculate the relevant timeframe on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: OIC 24: Offer In Compromise, 24 months to act!

questions.question: A taxpayer owes $28,000 in back taxes and has monthly income of $2,800 with monthly living expenses of $2,750. The taxpayer has no significant assets. What IRS collection alternative is most appropriate?
options: Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status because the taxpayer cannot pay and meet basic living expenses
An installment agreement with monthly payments of $50
An offer in compromise based on doubt as to collectibility
Voluntary bankruptcy filing to discharge the tax debt
questions.explanation: Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is appropriate when a taxpayer's allowable expenses meet or exceed income, making collection impractical. With only $50 monthly surplus, the IRS may classify the account as CNC. While in CNC status, the IRS suspends active collection efforts but penalties and interest continue to accrue, and the collection statute (CSED) continues to run. The IRS periodically reviews CNC accounts for changed circumstances.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is the most appropriate option because the taxpayer's monthly expenses nearly equal their income, leaving a negligible amount for tax payments. The IRS will generally grant CNC status when a taxpayer demonstrates an inability to pay without sacrificing basic living expenses, as active collection efforts would be unproductive.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An installment agreement with $50 monthly payments is unrealistic and unlikely to be accepted by the IRS, given the $28,000 debt and the taxpayer's limited ability to pay. The IRS generally requires installment agreements to be based on the taxpayer's ability to pay the debt within the collection statute of limitations, and $50 per month would not make a significant impact.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An Offer in Compromise (OIC) based on doubt as to collectibility is premature. While the taxpayer has limited income and assets, an OIC requires a thorough investigation of the taxpayer's financial situation and a determination that the IRS cannot collect the full amount of the tax liability within the remaining time. CNC status should be explored first, as it's a less complex process.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Voluntary bankruptcy filing is a drastic measure and not the most appropriate initial step. While bankruptcy can discharge certain tax debts, it has significant legal and financial consequences. Other collection alternatives, such as CNC status, should be explored before considering bankruptcy. Furthermore, certain tax debts, such as those arising from fraud or willful evasion, are generally not dischargeable in bankruptcy.

questions.educational: The IRS offers various collection alternatives to assist taxpayers facing financial hardship. Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is a temporary suspension of collection activity when a taxpayer's income is insufficient to cover basic living expenses. Understanding these alternatives, including installment agreements and Offers in Compromise, is crucial for EAs to effectively represent clients and navigate the complexities of IRS collection procedures, as outlined in Circular 230.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully analyze the taxpayer's income, expenses, and assets to determine the most suitable collection alternative. CNC status is often the best initial approach when a taxpayer demonstrates an inability to pay.
questions.memoryAid: CNC = Can't Collect Now

questions.question: CNC status may be granted when:
options: Monthly income does not exceed allowable living expenses
The liability is for current year taxes only
The taxpayer does not want to pay
The taxpayer has filed all returns
questions.explanation: CNC is granted when the taxpayer has no ability to pay - monthly income equals or is less than allowable necessary expenses, and there are no assets to levy.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - CNC, or Currently Not Collectible, status is granted when a taxpayer's monthly income is insufficient to cover necessary living expenses, leaving no funds available to pay their tax debt. This determination is based on a thorough review of the taxpayer's financial situation, including income, expenses, and assets.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - CNC status is not limited to current year taxes only. It can apply to tax liabilities from multiple years if the taxpayer meets the financial hardship criteria. The age of the tax liability is not a determining factor for CNC status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A taxpayer's unwillingness to pay is not a valid reason for granting CNC status. CNC status is based solely on the taxpayer's inability to pay due to financial hardship, regardless of their willingness to fulfill their tax obligations. The IRS requires objective financial data to support a CNC determination.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While filing all returns is generally a prerequisite for working with the IRS, it is not the sole determinant for CNC status. A taxpayer must also demonstrate an inability to pay their tax debt due to insufficient income and assets, even if they have filed all required returns.

questions.educational: The Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is a temporary suspension of collection activity by the IRS due to a taxpayer's financial hardship. This status is not a forgiveness of the debt, and the IRS may resume collection efforts when the taxpayer's financial situation improves. Understanding CNC is crucial for Enrolled Agents as it allows them to advocate for clients facing severe financial difficulties and navigate the IRS's collection procedures effectively, a key aspect of representation before the IRS.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that CNC status is a temporary collection hold, not a permanent discharge of the tax debt. The IRS can resume collection activity if the taxpayer's financial situation improves.
questions.memoryAid: CNC: Can't Collect Now

questions.question: While an account is in CNC status:
options: The CSED is extended by 2 years
questions.explanation: CNC status does not suspend the collection statute. The 10-year CSED continues to run, and if it expires while the account is in CNC, the liability is written off.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - CNC status does not suspend the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). The IRS's ability to collect the tax liability continues to run even while the account is deemed uncollectible.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - CNC status does not create a new CSED. The original CSED remains in effect, and the IRS must collect the tax liability before it expires.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) continues to run even when an account is placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status. This means the IRS's legal authority to collect the tax liability expires on the original CSED, regardless of the CNC status.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - CNC status does not automatically extend the CSED by two years or any other specific period. The CSED remains unchanged unless specific actions, such as an offer in compromise or installment agreement request, cause it to be suspended.

questions.educational: The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) is a critical concept for Enrolled Agents. It represents the 10-year period during which the IRS can legally collect a tax liability. Understanding how various actions, like CNC status, installment agreements, or offers in compromise, affect the CSED is essential for advising clients and protecting their interests.
questions.examTip: Remember that CNC status is a temporary administrative designation based on a taxpayer's inability to pay; it does not alter the underlying legal timeframe for collection. Pay close attention to questions involving the CSED and how it interacts with other IRS procedures.
questions.memoryAid: CNC doesn't stop the clock! (CSED)

questions.question: While in CNC status:
options: Interest and penalties continue to accrue
questions.explanation: Even while in CNC status, penalties and interest continue to accrue on the tax liability. The balance will grow while collection is suspended.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - CNC status only suspends collection activity, it does not abate the underlying tax liability. Interest and penalties continue to accrue, increasing the total amount owed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - CNC status provides temporary relief from enforced collection actions, but the underlying tax liability remains. Both interest and penalties continue to accrue during the CNC period.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A taxpayer's placement in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is a temporary suspension of collection activity due to financial hardship. While the IRS will not actively pursue collection, the tax liability, including accrued interest and penalties, continues to grow until the debt is resolved.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - CNC status does not stop either interest or penalties from accruing. Both continue to accumulate on the outstanding tax liability.

questions.educational: The IRS grants CNC status to taxpayers who demonstrate an inability to pay their tax debt due to significant financial hardship. This status prevents enforced collection actions like levies and garnishments, but it's crucial to understand that the underlying tax liability, including interest and penalties, continues to accrue. This is because the tax debt is still legally owed, and the government is entitled to compensation for the delayed payment and any noncompliance.
questions.examTip: Remember that CNC status is a temporary collection suspension, not forgiveness. On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between actions that abate a tax liability versus those that merely postpone collection.
questions.memoryAid: CNC = Collections are paused, Charges continue.

questions.question: When an account is placed in CNC status:
options: All liens are converted to judgments
Any federal tax lien is released
The federal tax lien typically remains in place
A new lien is automatically filed
questions.explanation: Federal tax liens generally remain in place when an account goes into CNC status. The lien protects the government's interest while collection is suspended.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Placing an account in CNC status does not automatically convert liens to judgments. A judgment is a court order, while a lien is a claim against property.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A federal tax lien is not automatically released when an account is placed in CNC status. Releasing the lien would jeopardize the government's secured interest in the taxpayer's property.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - When an account is placed in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status, the IRS has determined that the taxpayer cannot afford to pay the tax debt at that time. However, the federal tax lien typically remains in place to protect the government's interest in the taxpayer's property should their financial situation improve.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A new lien is not automatically filed when an account is placed in CNC status. If a lien already exists, it remains in place. If a lien hasn't been filed, CNC status doesn't trigger an automatic filing.

questions.educational: Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is a temporary suspension of collection activity due to the taxpayer's inability to pay. The IRS uses CNC status to balance its duty to collect taxes with its responsibility to consider a taxpayer's financial hardship. While collection actions are suspended, the underlying tax liability, including penalties and interest, continues to accrue, and the lien remains in place to secure the government's claim.
questions.examTip: Remember that CNC status is a temporary reprieve, not forgiveness. The IRS can resume collection activity if the taxpayer's financial situation improves, and the lien remains in effect until the debt is satisfied or the statute of limitations expires, so be prepared to advise clients on long-term strategies.
questions.memoryAid: CNC = Collection NOT Cancelled (lien stays).

questions.question: CNC closing code 03 indicates:
questions.explanation: CNC closing code 03 is "hardship" - the taxpayer has been determined unable to pay any amount due to financial circumstances. Other codes include 05 (unable to locate), 06 (deceased), etc.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - CNC closing code 05 indicates the IRS was unable to locate the taxpayer. This is a separate reason for closing a case as currently not collectible and involves a lack of contact, not a financial hardship.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - CNC closing code 06 indicates the taxpayer is deceased. While death certainly impacts collectibility, the specific code for a hardship situation due to financial inability to pay is different.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Account transfers are generally handled through internal IRS processes and do not typically result in a CNC status. A CNC status reflects the taxpayer's inability to pay, not a change in account ownership or location.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - CNC closing code 03 specifically signifies that the IRS has determined the taxpayer is experiencing a financial hardship and is currently unable to pay their tax liability. This determination is based on an evaluation of the taxpayer's income, expenses, and assets, as outlined in IRS procedures for hardship cases.

questions.educational: The Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is a temporary suspension of collection activity due to a taxpayer's inability to pay. The IRS uses various closing codes to categorize why a case is placed in CNC status. Understanding these codes is crucial for enrolled agents because it directly impacts how you advise clients facing financial difficulties and navigate IRS collection procedures on their behalf. The CNC status is not a forgiveness of the debt, and the IRS may resume collection activity if the taxpayer's financial situation improves.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that CNC status is a temporary suspension, not a permanent resolution. Be prepared to differentiate between the various CNC closing codes and their implications for your client's case.
questions.memoryAid: CNC-03: Can't Collect, Oh the Hardship!

questions.question: CNC accounts may be:
options: Reviewed periodically based on the taxpayer's financial situation
questions.explanation: The IRS periodically reviews CNC accounts. If the taxpayer's financial situation improves (increased income detected), the case may be reactivated for collection.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - CNC accounts are not 'set and forget.' The IRS has a responsibility to periodically review these accounts to determine if the taxpayer's financial situation has improved, allowing for collection efforts to resume.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS's policy is to periodically review CNC accounts to assess the taxpayer's current financial status. This is crucial because a taxpayer's circumstances can change, and the IRS needs to determine if they are now able to make payments on their tax debt. This review is based on factors like income, assets, and expenses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the IRS may eventually close a CNC account, it is not an automatic process after 3 years. The decision to close the account depends on various factors, including the age of the debt, the taxpayer's ongoing financial situation, and the likelihood of future collection.
