Understanding the exceptions to this rule, such as substantial omissions of income or fraud, is crucial for enrolled agents in advising clients and representing them before the IRS.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 3-year statute of limitations is the *general* rule. Always consider whether any exceptions (e.g., substantial omission of income, fraud, failure to file) might extend the assessment period when advising clients or preparing for the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think '3 years to see if the IRS agrees with me!'

questions.question: After an IRS examination, the "30-day letter" provides the taxpayer with:
options: Notice that the case has been referred for criminal prosecution
A proposed adjustment and the right to request an Appeals conference
A final assessment with no appeal rights
questions.explanation: The 30-day letter is sent after an IRS examination results in proposed changes. It includes the examination report detailing proposed adjustments and informs the taxpayer of their right to agree with the findings, request a conference with the IRS Appeals office within 30 days, or take no action (in which case a statutory notice of deficiency [90-day letter] will follow).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A referral for criminal prosecution is a separate and much more serious matter than a standard examination adjustment. This would not be communicated via a 30-day letter.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The 30-day letter is a key step in the IRS examination process, offering the taxpayer a chance to dispute proposed adjustments before a formal assessment. It outlines the proposed changes to the taxpayer's return and informs them of their right to request a conference with the IRS Appeals office, as detailed in IRS Publication 556.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 30-day letter precedes a final assessment. The taxpayer still has appeal rights at this stage, specifically the right to request an Appeals conference. A final assessment typically follows a 90-day letter (Notice of Deficiency) if the taxpayer doesn't respond or agree.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A refund notification is issued when the IRS determines that the taxpayer is due a refund, not after an examination that results in proposed adjustments increasing the tax liability.

questions.educational: The 30-day letter is a critical communication from the IRS following an examination. It's designed to give taxpayers a chance to understand proposed adjustments and exercise their right to appeal before the IRS takes further action. Understanding the examination process and appeal rights is crucial for enrolled agents to effectively represent their clients and navigate tax controversies.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the 30-day letter is the taxpayer's opportunity to engage with the IRS Appeals office before a formal assessment, so understanding the Appeals process is key.
questions.memoryAid: 30-day letter: 'A'ppeals 'A'vailable

questions.question: Under Circular 230, a practitioner's advertising must:
options: Include a guarantee of the lowest tax liability
Not be false, fraudulent, coercive, or misleading
questions.explanation: Under Circular 230 §10.30, a practitioner may advertise professional services, but all advertising must not be false, fraudulent, coercive, or misleading. Practitioners may not make claims of special consideration or relationship with the IRS. They may describe educational/professional background and use the designation "Enrolled Agent" or "EA" if properly enrolled.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Circular 230 explicitly prohibits practitioners from making guarantees regarding tax liability. Promising the "lowest tax liability" is misleading and could be interpreted as a guarantee of a specific outcome, which is a violation of ethical advertising standards.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Circular 230 §10.30(a)(1) clearly states that a practitioner "may not use any form of public communication or private solicitation containing a false, fraudulent, coercive, or misleading statement or claim." This is the core principle governing advertising by tax professionals.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS does not pre-approve advertising by practitioners. Requiring pre-approval would be an administrative burden and is not part of the IRS's oversight process. The responsibility for ensuring compliance with Circular 230 rests with the practitioner.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Circular 230 does not restrict advertising to print media. Practitioners can advertise through various channels, including online platforms, radio, television, and print. The key requirement is that the advertising is not false, fraudulent, coercive, or misleading, regardless of the medium used.

questions.educational: Circular 230 aims to protect taxpayers and maintain the integrity of the tax system. By prohibiting false or misleading advertising, it ensures that taxpayers receive accurate information and are not deceived by practitioners making unsubstantiated claims. This aligns with the broader ethical responsibilities of Enrolled Agents to act with honesty and integrity in their dealings with clients and the IRS, as outlined in Circular 230.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Circular 230 focuses on protecting taxpayers from misleading or unethical practices. When evaluating advertising scenarios, always consider whether the claims made are truthful and substantiated.
questions.memoryAid: Think of the acronym FFC: Advertising must NOT be False, Fraudulent, or Coercive.

questions.question: When a taxpayer files a new Form 2848 for the same tax matter, the new form:
options: Has no effect on prior authorizations
Automatically revokes any prior power of attorney for the same tax matter unless the taxpayer checks the box to retain prior representatives
Must be accompanied by a formal revocation letter
questions.explanation: Filing a new Form 2848 automatically revokes any prior Power of Attorney on file for the same tax matter/period unless the taxpayer specifically checks the box on line 6 to retain earlier powers of attorney. A taxpayer may also revoke a power of attorney by submitting a written statement of revocation or by submitting a new Form 2848 with a "REVOKE" notation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing a new Form 2848 generally *does* affect prior authorizations. Unless the taxpayer indicates otherwise, the new form revokes previous powers of attorney for the same tax matter.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A new Form 2848 automatically revokes any prior power of attorney for the same tax matter unless the taxpayer specifically checks the box on line 6, 'Check this box if you want to retain the prior power(s) of attorney but revoke the prior representative(s).' This allows the taxpayer to control which representatives are authorized while avoiding confusion for the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a taxpayer *can* revoke a power of attorney with a separate written statement, it's not *required* when filing a new Form 2848. The new Form 2848 itself acts as a revocation of prior authorizations unless the retention box is checked.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 2848 does not need to be notarized. The taxpayer's signature under penalty of perjury is sufficient for the IRS to accept the form.

questions.educational: The Power of Attorney (POA) system is designed to allow taxpayers to authorize representatives to act on their behalf before the IRS. Form 2848 is the standard form used for this purpose. To prevent confusion and ensure clarity regarding who is authorized to represent a taxpayer, the IRS assumes that a new Form 2848 supersedes any prior authorizations unless explicitly stated otherwise by the taxpayer. This aligns with the Circular 230 requirement for practitioners to act with diligence and avoid conflicts of interest.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the specific language on Form 2848, especially line 6 regarding retention of prior powers of attorney. The EA exam often tests your ability to correctly interpret and apply the instructions on IRS forms.
questions.memoryAid: New 2848 = New Rules (unless you check the box!).

questions.question: A taxpayer who receives a Notice of Intent to Levy has how many days to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6330, a taxpayer who receives a Notice of Intent to Levy (or a Notice of Federal Tax Lien filing) has 30 days from the date of the notice to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing with the IRS Office of Appeals. At the CDP hearing, the taxpayer may raise issues including the appropriateness of collection actions, propose collection alternatives (installment agreement, OIC), and challenge the underlying liability (if not previously challenged).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 10-day period generally relates to the time a taxpayer has to pay a tax liability after notice and demand before the IRS can begin levy actions, not the time to request a CDP hearing.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6330(a)(2) clearly states that a taxpayer has 30 days from the date of a Notice of Intent to Levy to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing. This hearing allows the taxpayer to discuss collection alternatives and potentially challenge the underlying tax liability if they haven't previously had the opportunity.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 60 days is not the statutory timeframe for requesting a CDP hearing after a Notice of Intent to Levy. While some IRS notices have a 60-day response window, CDP hearings are specifically governed by the 30-day rule under IRC §6330.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 90 days is not the correct timeframe. The IRS provides a shorter window of 30 days to request a CDP hearing to expedite the collection process while still protecting taxpayer rights.

questions.educational: Collection Due Process (CDP) hearings are a critical taxpayer right designed to ensure fairness and transparency in the IRS collection process. They balance the IRS's need to collect taxes with the taxpayer's right to be heard and explore alternative payment options. Understanding CDP rights and procedures is essential for Enrolled Agents to effectively represent their clients before the IRS.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific type of IRS notice mentioned in the question, as different notices trigger different response deadlines and taxpayer rights. Memorize the 30-day window for CDP requests after a Notice of Intent to Levy or Notice of Federal Tax Lien filing.
questions.memoryAid: CDP = 30 Days (Think: Collection Due Process is a '30-day process' after the notice).

questions.question: Form 4549 (Income Tax Examination Changes) is commonly known as the:
questions.explanation: Form 4549 (Income Tax Examination Changes) is commonly referred to as the Revenue Agent Report (RAR). It details the IRS examiner's proposed changes to the taxpayer's return, including adjustments to income, deductions, credits, and the resulting change in tax liability. The taxpayer may agree with the changes by signing the form or request an Appeals conference.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A closing agreement (Form 866 or Form 906) is a binding agreement between the IRS and the taxpayer that conclusively settles a specific tax liability. Form 4549 is a preliminary report, not a final, binding agreement.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 4549, Income Tax Examination Changes, is the document used by IRS examiners to propose adjustments to a taxpayer's income tax return. It's commonly known as the Revenue Agent Report (RAR) and summarizes the examiner's findings, proposed changes, and the resulting tax impact. The taxpayer's agreement or disagreement with the RAR initiates further steps in the examination process.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A statutory notice of deficiency (commonly called a 90-day letter) is issued by the IRS when it determines a deficiency in tax and the taxpayer has not agreed to the proposed changes. It gives the taxpayer 90 days to petition the Tax Court. Form 4549 is issued earlier in the examination process, before a statutory notice of deficiency.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A tax lien notice (Notice of Federal Tax Lien) is filed by the IRS to publicly record the government's claim against a taxpayer's property for unpaid taxes. This occurs after a tax assessment has been made and the taxpayer has failed to pay. Form 4549 is related to the examination process, which precedes the assessment and collection stages.

questions.educational: The examination process is a key area for Enrolled Agents. Understanding the sequence of events, from initial contact to resolution, is crucial. Form 4549 is a critical document in this process, as it outlines the IRS's proposed adjustments and forms the basis for further discussion or appeal. Knowing the purpose and implications of each form, like the 4549, 870, and the Notice of Deficiency, is essential for effective taxpayer representation.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timing of different IRS forms and notices within the examination and appeals process. Knowing which form comes first can help you eliminate incorrect answer choices.
questions.memoryAid: RAR = Revenue Agent Report = Report of Adjustments

questions.question: Under Circular 230, if a practitioner discovers that a client has not complied with federal tax laws or has made an error or omission on a tax filing, the practitioner must:
options: Immediately report the error to the IRS
Promptly advise the client of the noncompliance, error, or omission and its consequences
Withdraw from the engagement without informing the client
File an amended return on the client's behalf without authorization
questions.explanation: Under Circular 230 §10.21, a practitioner who becomes aware that a client has not complied with the tax laws or has made an error or omission in any return must promptly advise the client of the fact and the consequences. The practitioner is not required to report the error to the IRS and may not do so without the client's consent (due to confidentiality), but must inform the client of the corrective options.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Circular 230 does not require the practitioner to immediately report the error to the IRS. Reporting the error without the client's consent would violate confidentiality rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Circular 230 §10.21 explicitly states that a practitioner must promptly advise the client of the noncompliance, error, or omission and its potential consequences under the law. This allows the client to make informed decisions about how to rectify the situation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While withdrawal from the engagement *may* be necessary in some situations (e.g., if the client refuses to correct the error), Circular 230 requires the practitioner to first advise the client of the issue. Simply withdrawing without informing the client is a violation of professional responsibilities.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Filing an amended return on the client's behalf without authorization is a violation of both Circular 230 and general ethical principles. Practitioners must obtain informed consent from the client before taking any action on their behalf.

questions.educational: Circular 230 emphasizes the practitioner's duty to advise clients on tax matters, promoting voluntary compliance with tax laws. This requirement balances the practitioner's responsibility to the tax system with their duty of confidentiality to the client. Understanding these ethical obligations is crucial for maintaining professional integrity and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Circular 230 prioritizes advising the client first. Reporting to the IRS or withdrawing should only be considered after the client has been informed and given the opportunity to correct the issue.
questions.memoryAid: A-C-A: Advise, Correct, Action. First Advise the client, then help them Correct the error, then take Action (if needed, like withdrawal) based on their response.

questions.question: To qualify for innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b), the requesting spouse must establish all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A joint return was filed with an understatement of tax attributable to the other spouse
The requesting spouse did not know and had no reason to know of the understatement at the time of signing
It would be inequitable to hold the requesting spouse liable
The request is filed within 1 year of the first collection activity
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6015(b), a spouse may request innocent spouse relief if: (1) a joint return was filed, (2) there was an understatement due to erroneous items of the other spouse, (3) the requesting spouse did not know and had no reason to know of the understatement, and (4) it would be inequitable to hold the requesting spouse liable. The request must be filed within 2 years (not 1 year) of the first collection activity.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A joint return with an understatement of tax attributable to the other spouse is indeed a requirement for innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b). The understatement must be due to erroneous items of the non-requesting spouse.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The requesting spouse's lack of knowledge, or reason to know, of the understatement is a critical element for innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b). If the requesting spouse knew or had reason to know, relief is generally denied.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Establishing that it would be inequitable to hold the requesting spouse liable for the understatement is a necessary condition for innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b). This considers factors like whether the requesting spouse significantly benefitted from the understatement.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The deadline for requesting innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b) is generally within 2 years of the first collection activity by the IRS, not 1 year. Therefore, this is the exception and the correct answer.

questions.educational: Innocent spouse relief under IRC §6015(b) is designed to protect a spouse who unknowingly signed a joint return with an understatement of tax attributable to the other spouse. The IRS wants to ensure fairness and prevent undue hardship when one spouse is unaware of the other's tax errors. Understanding the specific requirements and deadlines is crucial for effectively representing clients seeking this type of relief.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to deadlines when dealing with innocent spouse relief. The 2-year window from the first collection activity is a common trap on the EA exam, so memorize it!
questions.memoryAid: Innocent Spouse: '2 Years to Tears' (request relief within 2 years of collection activity to avoid tax tears).

questions.question: All paid tax return preparers must have a valid:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6109(a)(4) and Treas. Reg. §1.6109-2, all paid tax return preparers must have a valid Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN) issued by the IRS. The PTIN must be renewed annually. This requirement applies regardless of whether the preparer is an enrolled agent, CPA, attorney, or unenrolled preparer. The PTIN must appear on every return prepared.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While many CPAs prepare tax returns, a CPA license is not universally required for all paid tax return preparers. The PTIN is the baseline requirement for anyone who gets paid to prepare returns, regardless of other credentials.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6109(a)(4) and Treas. Reg. §1.6109-2 mandate that all paid tax return preparers must obtain and use a Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN). This number, issued by the IRS and renewed annually, identifies the individual responsible for preparing the return and ensures accountability.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While Enrolled Agents (EAs) are authorized to represent taxpayers before the IRS, holding an enrolled agent enrollment card is not a universal requirement for *all* paid tax return preparers. The PTIN is the minimum requirement, even for those who are not EAs.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While attorneys may prepare tax returns, a state bar membership is not a universal requirement for all paid tax return preparers. Like CPAs and EAs, attorneys preparing returns for compensation must also possess a valid PTIN.

questions.educational: The PTIN requirement was instituted to improve oversight and accountability within the tax preparation industry. By requiring all paid preparers to register with the IRS, the agency can better track preparers, enforce ethical standards, and reduce instances of fraud or error. This directly relates to Circular 230, which governs the ethical conduct of tax professionals, including PTIN holders.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the PTIN is the *minimum* requirement for paid preparers. Even if someone holds other credentials (CPA, attorney, EA), they still need a PTIN to prepare returns for compensation.
questions.memoryAid: PTIN = Paid Tax Identification Number. If you're PAID, you need a PTIN.

questions.question: An Offer in Compromise (OIC) may be submitted on which of the following grounds?
options: Doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, or effective tax administration
Only when the taxpayer has filed bankruptcy
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7122, an Offer in Compromise may be submitted on three grounds: (1) Doubt as to Liability — legitimate doubt the assessed tax is correct; (2) Doubt as to Collectibility — the taxpayer's assets and income are insufficient to pay the full amount; (3) Effective Tax Administration (ETA) — no doubt about liability or collectibility, but collection would create economic hardship or be detrimental to voluntary compliance.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While doubt as to liability is a valid ground for an Offer in Compromise (OIC), it is not the *only* ground. IRC §7122 allows for other reasons, making this option incomplete and therefore incorrect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7122 explicitly states that an Offer in Compromise (OIC) can be submitted based on doubt as to liability (the taxpayer disputes the tax assessment), doubt as to collectibility (the taxpayer cannot afford to pay the full amount), or effective tax administration (even if the taxpayer can pay, collection would cause economic hardship or be unfair). This option accurately reflects all three permissible grounds.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While doubt as to collectibility is a valid ground for an Offer in Compromise (OIC), it is not the *only* ground. IRC §7122 allows for other reasons, such as doubt as to liability or effective tax administration, making this option incomplete and therefore incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While bankruptcy can impact a taxpayer's ability to pay taxes and might lead to an Offer in Compromise, it is not a *prerequisite* for submitting an OIC. An OIC can be submitted regardless of bankruptcy status if one of the three grounds (doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, or effective tax administration) is met.

questions.educational: Offers in Compromise (OICs) are a critical tool for resolving tax liabilities when taxpayers face financial hardship or dispute the tax assessment. Understanding the three grounds for an OIC – doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, and effective tax administration – is essential for Enrolled Agents. This knowledge allows you to effectively represent clients before the IRS and explore all available options for resolving their tax issues, aligning with the SEE3 blueprint area focusing on representation and IRS procedures.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to keywords like 'only,' 'always,' and 'never' in OIC questions. Remember that an OIC is a negotiation with the IRS, and the IRS has broad discretion in accepting or rejecting offers.
questions.memoryAid: Remember the acronym 'DCE' for the three OIC grounds: Doubt as to Collectibility, Doubt as to Liability, and Effective Tax Administration.

questions.question: The application fee for an Offer in Compromise (for taxpayers not certified as low-income) is:
questions.explanation: The application fee for an OIC is $205, which must be submitted with Form 656 (Offer in Compromise). Low-income taxpayers (income at or below 250% of the federal poverty guidelines) are exempt from both the application fee and the initial payment requirement. The fee is nonrefundable even if the offer is rejected.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $43 is not the current application fee for an Offer in Compromise. This amount may be related to other IRS fees, but it is not applicable to OICs.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $105 is not the current application fee for an Offer in Compromise. While IRS fees can change, this amount is not associated with the OIC application process.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The application fee for an Offer in Compromise (OIC) is $205 for taxpayers who do not qualify as low-income. This fee is submitted with Form 656 and is nonrefundable, regardless of whether the offer is accepted or rejected by the IRS.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $500 is not the current application fee for an Offer in Compromise. While the IRS can impose significant tax liabilities, penalties, and interest, the OIC application fee is significantly lower.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program allows eligible taxpayers to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. The application fee helps offset the IRS's costs of processing and evaluating OICs. Understanding OIC requirements, including eligibility criteria and application fees, is crucial for Enrolled Agents advising clients facing tax debt.
questions.examTip: Remember that the OIC application fee is non-refundable, even if the offer is rejected, so carefully assess your client's eligibility before submitting Form 656 and the fee. Also, be prepared to discuss the low-income exception with clients.
questions.memoryAid: OIC fee? Think 'Two-Oh-See' ($205).

questions.question: A taxpayer who owes $45,000 or less in combined tax, penalties, and interest (for individual income tax) may qualify for a streamlined installment agreement without providing:
questions.explanation: The IRS offers a streamlined installment agreement for individual taxpayers who owe $50,000 or less (recently expanded from the historic $25,000 threshold) in combined tax, penalties, and interest for individual income tax. Under the streamlined process, the taxpayer does not need to submit a financial statement. The taxpayer must agree to pay the balance within 72 months or before the collection statute expires.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS requires a taxpayer's Social Security number for identification and to properly track tax liabilities, even under a streamlined installment agreement. This is a fundamental piece of information needed for processing.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - For streamlined installment agreements, taxpayers owing $50,000 or less in combined tax, penalties, and interest for individual income tax are not required to submit a financial statement (Form 433-A or 433-F). This is the key feature that distinguishes a streamlined agreement from a standard installment agreement, which requires a detailed financial assessment.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Proof of identity is essential for any agreement with the IRS to prevent fraud and ensure the correct taxpayer is entering into the agreement. The IRS will require verification of identity, even for streamlined agreements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A signed installment agreement request is necessary to formally initiate the installment agreement process. The taxpayer must agree to the terms and conditions of the agreement, which is documented through a signed request.

questions.educational: Streamlined installment agreements are designed to simplify the collection process for taxpayers with relatively small tax liabilities. By waiving the requirement for a financial statement, the IRS reduces the administrative burden on both the taxpayer and the agency, allowing for faster processing and resolution of tax debts. This aligns with the IRS's goal of efficient tax administration and taxpayer service, a key concept for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: Remember the $50,000 threshold for streamlined installment agreements. Be prepared to advise clients on whether they qualify and the implications of choosing this option versus a standard installment agreement, especially regarding the collection statute expiration.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Streamlined = Simple = No Financial Statement (Form 433)'

questions.question: A federal tax lien arises automatically when:
options: The IRS files a Notice of Federal Tax Lien
The IRS assesses the tax, sends a notice and demand for payment, and the taxpayer neglects or refuses to pay
The taxpayer files a return showing a balance due
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6321, a federal tax lien arises automatically when: (1) an assessment has been made, (2) the IRS sends a notice and demand for payment, and (3) the taxpayer neglects or refuses to pay within 10 days. The lien attaches to all property and rights to property of the taxpayer. The filing of a Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) is a separate action that makes the lien public.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The filing of a Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) is a subsequent action that perfects the lien and provides public notice to creditors. The lien itself arises *before* the NFTL is filed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6321 clearly states that a federal tax lien arises automatically when the IRS assesses the tax, sends a notice and demand for payment, and the taxpayer fails to pay. All three conditions must be met for the lien to attach to the taxpayer's property.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While filing a return showing a balance due indicates a tax liability, it doesn't automatically create a federal tax lien. The IRS must still assess the tax and issue a notice and demand for payment before a lien arises.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An audit is an examination of a taxpayer's return and doesn't automatically create a lien. A lien arises only after an assessment, notice and demand, and the taxpayer's failure to pay.

questions.educational: The federal tax lien is a critical tool for the IRS to collect unpaid taxes. It ensures the government has a priority claim against a taxpayer's assets. Understanding the precise conditions that trigger a lien is essential for advising clients on tax compliance and resolving tax debts, a core competency tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the three steps for a federal tax lien to arise: Assessment, Notice and Demand, and Failure to Pay. Knowing this sequence is crucial for answering lien-related questions on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: ANF: Assessment, Notice, Failure to Pay (to remember the order of events for a tax lien)

questions.question: Before the IRS may levy on a taxpayer's property, which of the following notices must generally be provided?
options: Only a verbal warning from the revenue officer
Notice and demand for payment, followed by a Notice of Intent to Levy at least 30 days before the levy
A court order approving the levy
Written approval from the taxpayer's representative
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6331, the IRS must provide: (1) a notice and demand for payment, (2) a Notice of Intent to Levy and Your Right to a Hearing (Letter 1058 or LT11) at least 30 days before the levy. The 30-day period gives the taxpayer time to pay, arrange payment, or request a Collection Due Process hearing. In jeopardy situations, the IRS may levy without the 30-day notice.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A verbal warning is insufficient. The IRS is required to provide formal written notices before levying on a taxpayer's property to ensure due process and provide a clear record of communication.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6331(d) mandates that the IRS provide a notice and demand for payment, followed by a Notice of Intent to Levy (Letter 1058 or LT11) at least 30 days before the levy. This 30-day period allows the taxpayer time to address the tax debt, explore payment options, or request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A court order is generally not required for the IRS to levy on a taxpayer's property. The IRS has the administrative authority to levy after providing the required notices, without needing prior judicial approval, except in specific circumstances.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While taxpayers can authorize representatives to act on their behalf, the IRS is not required to obtain written approval from the taxpayer's representative before levying. The IRS must still provide the required notices directly to the taxpayer.

questions.educational: The IRS's levy power is a significant tool for collecting taxes, but it's balanced by taxpayer rights and due process protections. The notice requirements ensure taxpayers are aware of the impending levy and have an opportunity to resolve the issue, request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing, or explore alternative payment arrangements, aligning with Circular 230's emphasis on fair and ethical representation.
questions.examTip: Remember the 30-day rule for the Notice of Intent to Levy. This is a frequently tested area, and understanding the exceptions (jeopardy situations) is also crucial for the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Demand then Declare' - Notice and Demand, then Notice of Intent to Levy.

questions.question: Which of the following property is generally exempt from IRS levy?
options: Unemployment benefits, certain annuity/pension payments, workers' compensation, and necessary clothing
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6334, certain property is exempt from levy, including: unemployment benefits, workers' compensation, certain public assistance and SSI payments, necessary clothing and schoolbooks, fuel and provisions for up to $6,250 (indexed), books and tools of a trade up to $3,125 (indexed), undelivered mail, necessary personal effects, and certain annuity and pension payments. A principal residence generally requires court approval.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Bank accounts are generally subject to levy by the IRS. While there may be hardship exceptions or situations where the IRS might choose not to levy a bank account, it is not generally exempt under IRC §6334.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6334 specifically lists unemployment benefits, workers' compensation, certain annuity and pension payments, and necessary clothing as property exempt from levy. These exemptions are designed to ensure taxpayers retain basic necessities and income sources to meet essential living expenses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Rental income is generally subject to levy by the IRS. It represents a source of income that can be seized to satisfy outstanding tax liabilities. While the IRS may consider the taxpayer's overall financial situation, rental income itself is not an exempt asset.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Investment accounts, such as brokerage accounts or retirement accounts (subject to certain exceptions), are generally subject to levy by the IRS. These accounts represent assets that can be liquidated to satisfy outstanding tax liabilities. While some retirement accounts may have protections, they are not universally exempt.

questions.educational: The IRS is granted broad authority to levy a taxpayer's property to satisfy unpaid tax liabilities. However, IRC §6334 provides specific exemptions to protect taxpayers from undue hardship. These exemptions reflect a balance between the government's need to collect taxes and the taxpayer's need to maintain a basic standard of living. Understanding these exemptions is crucial for enrolled agents advising clients facing IRS collection actions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of levy-related questions. The IRS has significant power to seize assets, but specific exemptions exist. Memorize the key exemptions under IRC §6334 to answer these questions correctly.
questions.memoryAid: Remember 'UNCLE' - Unemployment, Necessary Clothing, Certain Annuity/Pension, Living Expenses (fuel, provisions, etc.) - as a starting point for remembering levy exemptions.

questions.question: When the IRS places a taxpayer's account in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status, this means:
options: The IRS suspends collection activity because the taxpayer cannot pay, but the tax debt remains; interest and penalties continue to accrue
The taxpayer has 5 years to begin making payments
The statute of limitations on collection is paused
questions.explanation: Currently Not Collectible status means the IRS has temporarily suspended active collection because the taxpayer cannot afford to make payments. However, the tax debt is NOT forgiven — interest and penalties continue to accrue, the federal tax lien remains in place, and the IRS may refund-offset future refunds. The Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) continues to run, and the IRS periodically reviews CNC accounts.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - CNC status is a temporary suspension of collection activity, not forgiveness of the tax debt. The underlying tax liability, including principal, interest, and penalties, remains outstanding.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is granted when a taxpayer demonstrates an inability to pay their tax debt. While the IRS suspends active collection efforts, the debt is not forgiven, and interest and penalties continue to accrue until the debt is fully satisfied. This is a temporary measure, and the IRS periodically reviews CNC cases to assess the taxpayer's financial situation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - There is no automatic 5-year grace period before payments must begin when an account is placed in CNC status. The IRS will review the taxpayer's financial situation periodically and may request updated financial information to determine if the taxpayer's ability to pay has changed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the IRS suspends active collection efforts, the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) generally continues to run while the account is in CNC status. However, certain actions by the taxpayer, such as submitting an Offer in Compromise, can pause the CSED.

questions.educational: The IRS uses CNC status to manage its resources effectively by focusing collection efforts on taxpayers who have the ability to pay. This prevents the IRS from expending resources on cases where collection is unlikely in the short term. Understanding CNC status is crucial for EAs as it allows them to advise clients on managing tax debt when facing financial hardship and explore other resolution options like Offers in Compromise or installment agreements.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that CNC status is a temporary reprieve, not a permanent solution. Advise your clients that interest and penalties continue to accrue, and the IRS can still offset future refunds.
questions.memoryAid: CNC: Collection NOT Canceled (but temporarily chilled).

questions.question: The IRS generally has how many years from the date of assessment to collect a tax liability?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6502(a), the IRS has 10 years from the date of assessment to collect a tax liability. This is known as the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). After the CSED expires, the tax liability is legally unenforceable. The 10-year period may be extended by agreement (Form 900), while in bankruptcy, or during a CDP hearing. Certain actions (like an OIC) also suspend the CSED.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS does not have only 3 years to collect a tax liability after assessment. Three years is generally the statute of limitations for assessing a tax, not collecting it.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Five years is not the general statute of limitations for the IRS to collect a tax liability. While certain actions might trigger a shorter collection period in specific circumstances, the standard timeframe is longer.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Seven years is not the standard collection statute. The IRS has a longer period to pursue collection efforts after a tax liability has been assessed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §6502(a) provides that the IRS generally has 10 years from the date of assessment of a tax liability to collect that tax. This 10-year period is known as the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED), and after this date, the IRS can no longer legally enforce collection.

questions.educational: The 10-year collection statute exists to balance the government's need to collect revenue with the taxpayer's right to eventual closure on their tax obligations. Understanding the CSED is crucial for an Enrolled Agent because it directly impacts your ability to advise clients on their rights and options regarding outstanding tax liabilities, including strategies like Offers in Compromise or installment agreements that can affect the CSED.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates provided in scenarios involving tax collection; knowing the CSED and factors that can extend or suspend it is essential for determining the IRS's collection authority.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Collect TENaciously' - the IRS has 10 years to collect.

questions.question: A Statutory Notice of Deficiency (90-day letter) gives the taxpayer how many days to file a petition with the U.S. Tax Court?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6213(a), a taxpayer has 90 days from the date the Statutory Notice of Deficiency is mailed (150 days if addressed to a person outside the United States) to file a petition with the U.S. Tax Court. If the taxpayer does not petition within this period, the IRS may assess the deficiency. The Tax Court is the only forum where a taxpayer can challenge the deficiency without paying first.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The Statutory Notice of Deficiency, often called the 90-day letter, provides significantly more time than 30 days to petition the U.S. Tax Court. Choosing this option indicates a misunderstanding of the timeframe afforded to taxpayers to respond to the IRS's determination of a deficiency.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While 60 days is a longer period than 30, it's still insufficient time for a taxpayer to adequately prepare and file a petition with the U.S. Tax Court in response to a Notice of Deficiency. The IRS provides a longer timeframe to ensure taxpayers have ample opportunity to seek professional advice and compile necessary documentation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §6213(a) explicitly states that a taxpayer has 90 days from the date the Statutory Notice of Deficiency is mailed to file a petition with the U.S. Tax Court. This timeframe allows the taxpayer to dispute the IRS's determination of a tax deficiency before having to pay it.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While 150 days is the correct timeframe for taxpayers residing outside the United States, the question does not specify that the taxpayer is outside the U.S. Therefore, the general rule of 90 days applies. Selecting this option suggests overlooking the specific conditions outlined in the question.

questions.educational: The Statutory Notice of Deficiency (90-day letter) is a critical document because it's the IRS's formal notification that they believe a taxpayer owes additional taxes. The 90-day period (or 150 days for those outside the US) is a strict deadline; missing it means losing the opportunity to challenge the deficiency in Tax Court without first paying the tax. Understanding the rules surrounding the Notice of Deficiency is crucial for an Enrolled Agent to properly advise clients and protect their rights.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to details like the taxpayer's location when determining the deadline for filing a petition with the U.S. Tax Court. Remember the 90/150 day rule and that timely filing is essential to preserve the client's right to litigate in Tax Court without prepayment.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '90 days to say NO' to the IRS in Tax Court after receiving a Notice of Deficiency.

questions.question: Under a lump sum cash Offer in Compromise, the taxpayer must submit what amount with the offer?
options: 20% of the total offer amount with the application
No payment is required until the offer is accepted
questions.explanation: For a lump sum cash OIC (payable in 5 or fewer installments within 5 months of acceptance), the taxpayer must submit 20% of the total offer amount with Form 656 and the $205 application fee. For periodic payment OICs (payable in 6-24 months), the taxpayer must begin making proposed monthly payments while the offer is being considered. Low-income taxpayers are exempt from both the fee and the initial payment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Submitting the full offer amount upfront is not required for a lump-sum cash Offer in Compromise. The IRS only requires a percentage of the offer to be submitted with the application to demonstrate good faith.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - For a lump-sum cash Offer in Compromise (OIC), the taxpayer must submit 20% of the total offer amount with Form 656. This initial payment, along with the application fee (if applicable), shows the taxpayer's commitment to resolving their tax liability.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a percentage is required, 10% is not the correct amount for a lump-sum cash Offer in Compromise. The IRS specifically requires 20% of the offer amount to be submitted with the application.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Some payment is required with the offer for lump-sum cash OICs. Selecting this option would be incorrect, as the IRS requires a good faith payment to consider the offer seriously.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program allows taxpayers with financial difficulties to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. The IRS requires an initial payment with the offer to ensure the taxpayer is serious about fulfilling the terms of the agreement. This process is covered under IRC section 7122 and related regulations, which outline the requirements and procedures for OICs.
questions.examTip: Remember the different payment requirements for lump-sum cash OICs (20% upfront) versus periodic payment OICs (monthly payments during consideration) and the low-income exception to avoid confusion on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: Lump Sum = Large Sum (20%)

questions.question: A taxpayer may request copies of their own tax transcripts (e.g., account transcripts, return transcripts) by filing:
options: Form 4506-T (Request for Transcript of Tax Return)
questions.explanation: Taxpayers may request various types of tax transcripts by filing Form 4506-T (Request for Transcript of Tax Return) or by using the IRS online "Get Transcript" tool. Types include: return transcripts (showing most line items), account transcripts (showing account activity like payments and adjustments), record of account (combined), and wage and income transcripts. Full copies of actual returns require Form 4506 with a fee.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative, is used to authorize a representative (like an Enrolled Agent) to act on the taxpayer's behalf before the IRS. It does *not* allow the taxpayer to obtain their own tax transcripts.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 4506-T (Request for Transcript of Tax Return) is the correct form for a taxpayer to request various types of tax transcripts, including return transcripts, account transcripts, record of account, and wage and income transcripts. The IRS also provides an online "Get Transcript" tool for this purpose, but Form 4506-T is the paper-based method.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 8821, Tax Information Authorization, allows a taxpayer to authorize a third party (such as an Enrolled Agent) to *receive* their tax information from the IRS. It does not allow the taxpayer to obtain the information themselves.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request *could* potentially be used to obtain tax information, it is not the standard or most efficient method for a taxpayer to request their own tax transcripts. Form 4506-T is the specifically designed form for this purpose and is much faster.

questions.educational: Understanding how taxpayers can access their own tax information is crucial for representation. The IRS provides specific forms and online tools to streamline this process. Using the correct form (4506-T for transcripts, 4506 for full returns) ensures efficient processing and avoids unnecessary delays, which is important when preparing returns or representing clients in audits.
questions.examTip: Remember the difference between Form 2848 (Power of Attorney - representative acts *on behalf of* the client) and Form 8821 (Tax Information Authorization - representative *receives* information). Form 4506-T is for the taxpayer to get their *own* transcripts.
questions.memoryAid: 4506-*T* is for *Transcripts*; 2848 is for *Representation* (think 'R' for Representation); 8821 is for *Information* (think 'I' for Information).

questions.question: A certificate of subordination of a federal tax lien under IRC §6325(d) means:
options: The federal tax lien is completely released
The IRS makes the federal tax lien secondary to another creditor's interest
The taxpayer no longer owes the tax
The lien is transferred to another property
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6325(d), the IRS may issue a certificate of subordination, which makes the federal tax lien secondary (junior) to another creditor's lien. This is often requested when a taxpayer needs to refinance a mortgage — the mortgage lender requires that its lien be senior to the federal tax lien. The IRS will subordinate if doing so will ultimately facilitate collection of the tax (e.g., the taxpayer will use refinance proceeds to pay the tax).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A certificate of subordination does *not* release the lien entirely. It only changes the priority of the lien relative to other creditors. The tax debt remains outstanding, and the IRS still has a claim against the taxpayer's property.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6325(d) specifically allows the IRS to issue a certificate of subordination. This action places the federal tax lien in a lower priority position than another creditor's interest, such as a mortgage lender. This is done to facilitate actions like refinancing, which can ultimately benefit the IRS by improving the taxpayer's ability to pay the tax debt.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A certificate of subordination has absolutely no impact on the underlying tax liability. The taxpayer still owes the full amount of the tax, penalties, and interest. The subordination only affects the order in which creditors are paid from the taxpayer's assets.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Subordination does not involve transferring the lien to another property. The lien remains attached to the original property; only its priority relative to other liens is altered. A transfer of lien to another property would involve different procedures and documentation.

questions.educational: The IRS grants subordination to facilitate collection of the tax. By allowing a taxpayer to refinance, for example, the IRS hopes the taxpayer will use the proceeds to pay down the tax debt or improve their financial situation, making future payments more likely. Understanding lien priorities is crucial for EAs when advising clients on resolving tax debts and negotiating with the IRS.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS will only subordinate a lien if it believes doing so will ultimately increase the likelihood of collecting the tax debt. Consider this when advising clients on strategies involving subordination.
questions.memoryAid: SubORDINation makes the IRS lien 'ORDI-narily' lower in priority.

questions.question: A Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA) differs from a regular installment agreement in that:
options: It requires full payment within 60 months
The monthly payment amount will not fully satisfy the tax liability before the collection statute expires
It can only be requested by corporations
Interest stops accruing once the PPIA is approved
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6159(a), a Partial Payment Installment Agreement allows a taxpayer to make monthly payments that will not fully satisfy the tax liability before the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). The remaining balance is effectively uncollected after the CSED expires. The IRS must review the taxpayer's financial condition every 2 years. PPIAs require submission of Form 433-A or 433-F.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While regular installment agreements often aim for completion within a specific timeframe, a PPIA is specifically designed for situations where full payment within 60 months (or any timeframe before the CSED) is not feasible. The defining characteristic of a PPIA is the inability to fully pay the liability before the collection statute expires.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA), as defined under IRC §6159(a), is characterized by the fact that the agreed-upon monthly payments will not fully satisfy the outstanding tax liability before the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). This is the core difference between a PPIA and a standard installment agreement.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - PPIAs are not restricted to corporations. Individuals, businesses (including corporations), and other entities can potentially qualify for a PPIA if they meet the IRS's criteria based on their financial situation. The availability of a PPIA depends on the taxpayer's ability to pay, not their entity type.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Interest continues to accrue on the unpaid balance of a tax liability even after a PPIA is approved. The PPIA allows for a structured payment plan, but it does not halt the accrual of interest or penalties as outlined in IRC §6601 and related sections.

questions.educational: Partial Payment Installment Agreements (PPIAs) are a critical tool for representing clients who cannot fully satisfy their tax debt before the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED). Understanding the nuances of PPIAs, including the required financial disclosures (Form 433-A/F) and the IRS's periodic review process, is essential for effective tax resolution. This falls under the broader topic of collection alternatives, which also includes Offers in Compromise (OICs) and Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS will review a taxpayer's financial condition every two years under a PPIA. Be prepared to advise clients on maintaining accurate financial records and promptly responding to IRS requests for updated information to avoid default.
questions.memoryAid: PPIA = Partial Payment, Incomplete, Agreement (incomplete because it doesn't fully pay the debt before the CSED).

questions.question: Under the repetitive audit policy, the IRS generally will not examine the same items on a taxpayer's return if:
options: The taxpayer has already been audited once in their lifetime
Two or more prior examinations of the same issue resulted in no change or a small change
The return was prepared by a CPA
questions.explanation: Under IRM 4.10.2.7.2, the IRS policy on repetitive examinations provides that the IRS will not examine the same item(s) in a subsequent year if two or more prior examinations of the same issue resulted in a "no change" or only a small change. The taxpayer may notify the IRS examiner of prior no-change audits to invoke this protection.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A single prior audit, regardless of the outcome, does not trigger the repetitive audit policy. The IRS requires *two or more* prior examinations with no or small changes.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS's repetitive audit policy, as outlined in IRM 4.10.2.7.2, aims to avoid unnecessary taxpayer burden. If the same issue has been examined in two or more prior years and resulted in no change or only a small change, the IRS generally won't re-examine it.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The preparer of the return (CPA or otherwise) has no bearing on the IRS's repetitive audit policy. The policy focuses solely on the history of prior examinations of the *same issue*.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Taxpayers cannot simply request audit protection. The repetitive audit policy is triggered by a history of prior examinations with no or small changes, not by a taxpayer's request.

questions.educational: The repetitive audit policy balances the IRS's duty to ensure tax compliance with the need to avoid harassing taxpayers. It recognizes that repeatedly auditing the same issue with minimal or no adjustments is inefficient and burdensome. Understanding this policy is crucial for enrolled agents to effectively advocate for their clients during an IRS examination.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the repetitive audit policy requires *two or more* prior audits with no or small changes to be invoked; one prior audit is not enough.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Two or More, No More' - Two or more prior audits with no or small changes, then the IRS should examine the issue no more.

questions.question: Before contacting third parties (employers, banks, neighbors) regarding a taxpayer's account, the IRS must:
options: Provide the taxpayer with reasonable notice that third-party contacts may be made
Get written permission from the taxpayer
The IRS may contact anyone without notice
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7602(c), the IRS must provide the taxpayer with reasonable advance notice that contacts with third parties may be made. The notice must be provided at least 45 days before the third-party contact. The IRS must also periodically provide the taxpayer with a record of who was contacted. This protection was strengthened by the Taxpayer First Act of 2019.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A court order is not generally required for the IRS to contact third parties during an investigation. While judicial intervention can occur in certain circumstances, it's not the standard procedure for routine third-party contacts.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7602(c) mandates that the IRS provide taxpayers with reasonable notice before contacting third parties regarding their tax matters. This notice must be given at least 45 days in advance and includes a record of who was contacted, ensuring transparency and protecting taxpayer rights.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While taxpayer cooperation is often sought, the IRS is not required to obtain written permission from the taxpayer before contacting third parties. Reasonable notice, as defined in IRC §7602(c), is the legal requirement, not explicit written consent.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS cannot contact anyone without notice. IRC §7602(c) specifically prohibits this, requiring reasonable notice to the taxpayer before contacting third parties to protect taxpayer rights and ensure transparency in the investigation process.

questions.educational: The requirement for the IRS to provide notice before contacting third parties stems from taxpayer rights and due process considerations. This provision, strengthened by the Taxpayer First Act of 2019, aims to prevent abusive or overly intrusive investigative practices by ensuring taxpayers are informed and aware of the scope of the IRS's inquiry. Understanding taxpayer rights is crucial for Enrolled Agents as they advocate for their clients during IRS interactions.
questions.examTip: Remember the 45-day rule for third-party contact notification; this is a frequently tested detail on the EA exam, especially in scenarios involving IRS audits and investigations.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '45 days to notify' – the IRS gives you 45 days' notice before talking to your neighbors!

questions.question: When evaluating an Offer in Compromise based on doubt as to collectibility, the IRS calculates the taxpayer's Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) as:
options: Net realizable equity in assets plus future income (monthly disposable income × number of remaining statutory months)
Total assets at fair market value
questions.explanation: The Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) equals the net realizable equity (NRE) in the taxpayer's assets (quick sale value minus encumbrances and allowance) PLUS future income (monthly disposable income × the number of remaining months on the collection statute). For a lump sum offer, future income is multiplied by 12 months; for periodic payment, by 24 months. The IRS generally will not accept an OIC for less than the calculated RCP.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Total income minus total expenses represents a simplified view of cash flow, but it doesn't account for the taxpayer's assets or the time value of money. The IRS's RCP calculation is more comprehensive, considering both assets and future income potential over the collection statute period.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) is the IRS's assessment of the taxpayer's ability to pay, calculated as the sum of the net realizable equity in their assets (quick sale value less debts) and their future income. Future income is determined by multiplying monthly disposable income by the remaining months in the collection statute, typically 12 months for a lump sum offer or 24 months for a periodic payment offer.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Total assets at fair market value does not account for encumbrances (liens, mortgages) against those assets, nor does it consider the taxpayer's future income potential. The RCP calculation requires the *net realizable* equity, which is the fair market value less any debts secured by the asset and selling costs.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Annual gross income multiplied by 10 is an arbitrary calculation and bears no direct relationship to the IRS's method for determining Reasonable Collection Potential. The RCP calculation is based on a detailed analysis of the taxpayer's assets, liabilities, and future income stream.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program allows taxpayers to resolve their tax liabilities for less than the full amount owed. The IRS evaluates OICs based on doubt as to liability, doubt as to collectibility, or effective tax administration. Understanding the RCP calculation is crucial because the IRS generally won't accept an OIC for less than this amount, ensuring the government receives at least what it could reasonably collect.
questions.examTip: When preparing an Offer in Compromise for a client, meticulously document the valuation of assets and the calculation of monthly disposable income. The IRS will scrutinize these figures, and accurate documentation is essential for a successful OIC.
questions.memoryAid: RCP = NRE + FI (Reasonable Collection Potential = Net Realizable Equity + Future Income)

questions.question: Under IRC §6325(a), the IRS must release a Notice of Federal Tax Lien within 30 days after:
options: The liability is satisfied or becomes legally unenforceable, or the IRS accepts a bond
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6325(a), the IRS must release a federal tax lien within 30 days after: (1) the liability is fully satisfied or becomes legally unenforceable (CSED expiration), (2) a bond is accepted, or (3) the IRS determines that the lien was filed in error. The release does not remove the lien from public records; the taxpayer may request a Certificate of Release of Federal Tax Lien.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing an appeal does not automatically trigger the release of a Notice of Federal Tax Lien. While an appeal might delay collection activity, the lien remains in place until the underlying tax liability is satisfied or becomes legally unenforceable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6325(a) explicitly states that the IRS must release a Notice of Federal Tax Lien within 30 days after the tax liability is fully satisfied or becomes legally unenforceable (e.g., due to the Collection Statute Expiration Date - CSED), or if the IRS accepts a bond guaranteeing payment of the liability. This ensures the lien doesn't remain active when it's no longer needed to secure the government's interest.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a taxpayer can request a release of the lien, the IRS is only *required* to release it under the conditions specified in IRC §6325(a). A simple request, without meeting those conditions, does not obligate the IRS to release the lien.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The 5-year period is not directly related to the *release* of a federal tax lien. While the IRS generally has 10 years from the date of assessment to collect a tax liability (the CSED), the lien remains in effect until the liability is satisfied, becomes legally unenforceable, or a bond is accepted, regardless of whether 5 years have passed since the filing date.

questions.educational: The Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) protects the government's interest in a taxpayer's property when they owe taxes. The IRS files the NFTL to establish priority over other creditors. Understanding the conditions for releasing a lien is crucial for EAs because it directly impacts your ability to advise clients on resolving tax liabilities and protecting their assets. The CSED is a key concept here, as it represents the date beyond which the IRS can no longer legally collect the tax.
questions.examTip: Remember the 30-day timeframe for releasing a lien after satisfaction, unenforceability, or acceptance of a bond. On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific dates and conditions related to liens and levies, as these are frequently tested.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'SUE' for Satisfaction, Unenforceable, or Escrow (bond) – these trigger the lien release.

questions.question: Under IRC §7491(a), the burden of proof in a tax court proceeding shifts to the IRS if the taxpayer:
options: Introduces credible evidence, complies with substantiation requirements, cooperates with reasonable IRS requests, and maintains required records
Is represented by an enrolled agent
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7491(a), the burden of proof shifts to the IRS in court proceedings if the taxpayer: (1) introduces credible evidence with respect to the factual issue, (2) complies with substantiation and record-keeping requirements, (3) cooperates with reasonable IRS requests for information, documents, meetings, and interviews, and (4) maintains records. The burden of proof is always on the IRS for penalties and in cases involving individuals with net worth under $7 million.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While filing a petition within 90 days is crucial for accessing Tax Court, it doesn't directly trigger the burden of proof shift to the IRS under IRC §7491(a). The timeliness of the petition is a jurisdictional requirement, not a condition for shifting the burden of proof.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7491(a) explicitly states that the burden of proof shifts to the IRS if the taxpayer introduces credible evidence regarding a factual issue, complies with substantiation requirements under the IRC, cooperates with reasonable IRS requests for information, documents, meetings, and interviews, and maintains all records required under the IRC. Meeting all these conditions allows the taxpayer to benefit from the burden shift.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While there are provisions that may favor taxpayers with smaller tax liabilities, a tax liability under $50,000 alone does not automatically shift the burden of proof to the IRS under IRC §7491(a). The shift depends on the taxpayer meeting the conditions outlined in option B.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Being represented by an enrolled agent does not, by itself, shift the burden of proof to the IRS. While representation is beneficial, the taxpayer must still meet the specific requirements of IRC §7491(a), such as introducing credible evidence and cooperating with the IRS.

questions.educational: IRC §7491(a) aims to balance the power between the IRS and taxpayers in tax disputes. By requiring taxpayers to provide credible evidence and cooperate, it ensures that the IRS cannot rely solely on presumptions without substantiating their claims. This provision is particularly relevant in the Examination Process (SEE3-2) as it dictates how evidence is presented and who bears the responsibility of proving a tax liability.
questions.examTip: Remember that the burden of proof shift under IRC §7491(a) is conditional; simply having a good case or being represented is insufficient. As an Enrolled Agent, you must ensure your client meticulously documents their position and cooperates fully with the IRS to potentially benefit from this provision.
questions.memoryAid: C-SCRIM: Credible evidence, Substantiation, Cooperation, Records, IRS bears burden of proof if taxpayer meets these.

questions.question: An installment agreement may be terminated by the IRS if the taxpayer:
options: Files a return showing a refund
Fails to make a payment, fails to file a required return, or fails to provide updated financial information
Earns income from a new employer
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6159(b)(5), the IRS may terminate an installment agreement if the taxpayer: (1) fails to make a timely payment, (2) fails to file a required return or pay current taxes, (3) fails to provide updated financial information when requested, or (4) provides inaccurate or incomplete information. Before termination, the IRS must provide 30 days' notice and an opportunity to cure the default.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing a return showing a refund does not automatically terminate an installment agreement. In fact, the IRS may apply the refund to the outstanding tax liability, which would be beneficial.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6159(b)(5) explicitly states that the IRS can terminate an installment agreement if the taxpayer fails to make a timely payment, fails to file a required tax return, or fails to provide updated financial information when requested. These actions indicate a potential inability or unwillingness to comply with the agreement's terms.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Simply changing a mailing address does not automatically terminate an installment agreement. Taxpayers are required to notify the IRS of address changes, and the IRS will update their records accordingly. While failing to notify the IRS of an address change could lead to missed notices and subsequent issues, the address change itself is not grounds for termination.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Earning income from a new employer, in itself, does not trigger termination of an installment agreement. The IRS is primarily concerned with timely payments and compliance with filing requirements, not the source of income.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a collection alternative that allows taxpayers to pay their tax liabilities over time. The IRS grants these agreements based on the taxpayer's ability to pay, and continued compliance is crucial. Failure to adhere to the terms of the agreement, such as timely payments and filing requirements, undermines the basis upon which the agreement was granted, justifying its termination. Understanding collection alternatives and the conditions for their termination is vital for EAs advising clients facing tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS must provide a 30-day notice and an opportunity to cure the default before terminating an installment agreement. As an EA, you should advise your clients to respond promptly to any IRS notices regarding potential termination.
questions.memoryAid: FIT: Failure to File, Installment (payment) failure, or Transparency (financial info) failure can terminate an installment agreement.

questions.question: A taxpayer may request penalty abatement based on:
options: Reasonable cause, first-time penalty abatement, or statutory exception
Having an enrolled agent as their representative
questions.explanation: Penalties may be abated for: (1) Reasonable cause — the taxpayer exercised ordinary business care and prudence but was unable to comply (death, serious illness, fire, natural disaster, reliance on erroneous IRS advice); (2) First-time penalty abatement (FTA) — administrative waiver for taxpayers with a clean compliance history for the prior 3 years; (3) Statutory exceptions — specific provisions in the Code. The taxpayer must provide supporting documentation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While financial hardship can be a factor considered under reasonable cause, it is not the *only* basis for penalty abatement. Taxpayers can also request abatement based on first-time penalty abatement or statutory exceptions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Penalties can be abated for reasonable cause (demonstrating ordinary business care and prudence but inability to comply), first-time penalty abatement (FTA) for taxpayers with a clean compliance history, or statutory exceptions as outlined in the Internal Revenue Code. Taxpayers must substantiate their claims with supporting documentation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While having an enrolled agent as a representative can *assist* a taxpayer in preparing and presenting a penalty abatement request, the presence of an EA does not automatically guarantee or serve as a basis for penalty abatement. The abatement is based on the merits of the case, not the representative's credentials.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Filing an extension to file a tax return prevents the failure-to-file penalty if the return is filed by the extended due date and the tax is paid. However, simply filing an extension does not provide a basis for abating other penalties, such as the failure-to-pay penalty if the tax was not paid by the original due date.

questions.educational: The IRS imposes penalties to encourage compliance with tax laws. However, recognizing that circumstances can prevent timely compliance despite a taxpayer's best efforts, the IRS provides avenues for penalty abatement. Understanding the grounds for abatement, such as reasonable cause, is crucial for EAs to effectively advocate for their clients and ensure fair application of tax laws. This connects to the broader exam topics of taxpayer rights and IRS procedures.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that 'reasonable cause' requires demonstrating that the taxpayer exercised ordinary business care and prudence but was still unable to comply due to circumstances beyond their control. Document everything!
questions.memoryAid: RFS: Remember the three main reasons for penalty abatement: Reasonable cause, First-time abatement, and Statutory exception.

questions.question: A closing agreement under IRC §7121 is:
options: An informal letter from the IRS closing the examination
A final, binding agreement between the IRS and the taxpayer on a specific tax matter that cannot be reopened
An agreement to extend the statute of limitations
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7121, a closing agreement is a final determination of a taxpayer's tax liability for a specific matter or period. It is binding on both the IRS and the taxpayer and generally cannot be reopened, modified, or set aside except upon a showing of fraud, malfeasance, or misrepresentation of a material fact. Closing agreements may cover specific issues (Form 906) or an entire tax year (Form 866).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - An informal letter from the IRS closing an examination is not a closing agreement. A closing agreement is a formal, legally binding agreement, not a simple letter.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7121 defines a closing agreement as a final and conclusive agreement between the IRS and a taxpayer regarding a specific tax liability or matter. These agreements are binding and can only be reopened under very limited circumstances, such as fraud or misrepresentation of a material fact.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An agreement to extend the statute of limitations is a separate agreement, typically achieved using Form 872, Consent to Extend the Time to Assess Tax. This extends the period the IRS has to assess tax, whereas a closing agreement settles a specific tax matter definitively.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A power of attorney (POA), typically Form 2848, authorizes a representative to act on behalf of a taxpayer before the IRS. It does not resolve or close out a tax matter like a closing agreement does.

questions.educational: Closing agreements are powerful tools used to achieve certainty in complex tax situations. They are often used after audits or when there are disagreements about the interpretation of tax law. Understanding the finality and limited recourse associated with closing agreements is crucial, as they represent a binding commitment from both the taxpayer and the IRS.
questions.examTip: Remember that closing agreements are rarely reopened; therefore, advise your clients to seek expert counsel before entering into one. On the exam, pay close attention to the specific facts presented to determine if a closing agreement is the appropriate course of action.
questions.memoryAid: Closing Agreements = Certainty. Think 'CA=C' to remember that closing agreements provide certainty and finality.

questions.question: A taxpayer may request a discharge of a federal tax lien from specific property under IRC §6325(b) if:
options: The taxpayer files a new return
The property's value is double the lien amount, or the remaining property/interests are sufficient to cover the liability
The property is the taxpayer's primary residence
The taxpayer has made 6 consecutive installment payments
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6325(b), the IRS may discharge specific property from the federal tax lien if: (1) part of the liability is paid (equal to the property's value), (2) the remaining property subject to the lien is at least double the remaining liability, (3) an alternative security is provided (bond or deposit), or (4) it is in the best interest of the IRS and will not jeopardize collection. Property must be appraised to determine value.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing a new return does not automatically trigger a discharge of a federal tax lien. While filing an amended return might impact the underlying tax liability, it's not directly related to lien discharge under IRC §6325(b).
