whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An ERO is not required to retain a copy of the taxpayer's driver's license. While verifying the taxpayer's identity is important, the e-file authorization form serves as the primary record of authorization, not a copy of their driver's license.

questions.educational: The requirement for EROs to retain e-file authorization forms (Form 8879 or 8453) for three years is rooted in Circular 230 and IRS regulations regarding electronic filing. This ensures accountability and provides a verifiable record of the taxpayer's consent to electronically file their return. This requirement helps to prevent fraud and unauthorized filings, protecting both the taxpayer and the IRS. Understanding record retention requirements is crucial for maintaining ethical and compliant tax practices as an Enrolled Agent.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to questions about record retention requirements for EROs. Remember the '3-year rule' for e-file authorization forms and distinguish it from other record-keeping responsibilities that may fall on the taxpayer or the IRS.
questions.memoryAid: ERO's E-file Authorization: Remember '8879/8453 for 3!' (Years)

questions.question: Under IRC §6695(g), the due diligence penalty for failure to meet due diligence requirements applies to which of the following credits?
options: Earned Income Credit (EIC), Child Tax Credit (CTC/ACTC/ODC), American Opportunity Credit (AOTC), and Head of Household filing status
All credits claimed on the return
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6695(g), the due diligence penalty of $560 per failure (for 2024 returns) applies per return for each of: (1) the Earned Income Credit (EIC), (2) the Child Tax Credit/Additional Child Tax Credit/Other Dependent Credit (CTC/ACTC/ODC), (3) the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC), and (4) Head of Household filing status. A preparer who fails to meet due diligence for all four on one return could face a penalty of $2,240.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The due diligence penalty under IRC §6695(g) applies to more than just the Child Tax Credit. It also includes the Earned Income Credit, American Opportunity Tax Credit, and Head of Household filing status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6695(g) specifically outlines that the due diligence requirements and associated penalties apply to preparers claiming the Earned Income Credit (EIC), Child Tax Credit/Additional Child Tax Credit/Other Dependent Credit (CTC/ACTC/ODC), American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC), and Head of Household filing status. Preparers must meet specific knowledge, inquiry, and record retention requirements for these items.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the Earned Income Credit is included in the due diligence requirements under IRC §6695(g), it is not the only credit or filing status subject to these rules. The Child Tax Credit, American Opportunity Tax Credit, and Head of Household filing status are also included.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The due diligence penalty under IRC §6695(g) does not apply to *all* credits claimed on a return. It is specifically limited to the Earned Income Credit, Child Tax Credit/Additional Child Tax Credit/Other Dependent Credit, American Opportunity Tax Credit, and Head of Household filing status.

questions.educational: The due diligence requirements for tax preparers related to EIC, CTC/ACTC/ODC, AOTC, and Head of Household filing status are in place to ensure preparers make reasonable inquiries and have a reasonable basis for claiming these benefits. This helps to reduce improper claims and protect the integrity of the tax system. Understanding these requirements is crucial for Enrolled Agents, as failure to comply can result in penalties under IRC §6695(g) and potential disciplinary action under Circular 230.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the specific credits and filing status subject to the due diligence penalty under IRC §6695(g): EIC, CTC/ACTC/ODC, AOTC, and Head of Household. A question might test your knowledge of which items are *not* included.
questions.memoryAid: E-CHAT: Earned Income Credit, Child Tax Credit, American Opportunity Tax Credit, Head of Household.

questions.question: Tax return preparers must complete Form 8867 (Paid Preparer's Due Diligence Checklist) to demonstrate due diligence for which purpose?
options: To document that they asked sufficient questions and obtained information to determine eligibility for EIC, CTC, AOTC, and Head of Household status
To register as an e-file provider
To request an extension of time
questions.explanation: Form 8867 must be completed and submitted with or retained for each return claiming the EIC, CTC/ACTC/ODC, AOTC, or Head of Household filing status. The form documents that the preparer: (1) completed the applicable worksheets, (2) asked the taxpayer sufficient questions, (3) documented the answers, (4) verified the information, and (5) did not know or have reason to know any information was incorrect. The form must be filed electronically with the return.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 8867 is not used to verify the taxpayer's identity. While preparers have a general due diligence obligation to ascertain the accuracy of information provided by the taxpayer, identity verification is a separate process, often involving reviewing documents like driver's licenses or social security cards.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 8867, the Paid Preparer's Due Diligence Checklist, is specifically designed to document that a tax return preparer has exercised due diligence in determining a taxpayer's eligibility for the Earned Income Credit (EIC), Child Tax Credit (CTC/ACTC/ODC), American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC), and Head of Household filing status. The form requires the preparer to confirm they asked appropriate questions, documented the answers, and reviewed relevant information to ensure the taxpayer meets the requirements for these tax benefits.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Registering as an e-file provider involves a separate application process with the IRS and does not involve Form 8867. E-file registration focuses on the preparer's ability to securely transmit tax returns electronically and adhere to IRS guidelines for electronic filing.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 8867 is not used to request an extension of time to file a tax return. Form 4868, Application for Automatic Extension of Time to File U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, is used for that purpose.

questions.educational: The due diligence requirements for tax preparers, particularly concerning credits like the EIC and CTC, are in place to minimize errors and fraud. The IRS holds preparers accountable for ensuring taxpayers meet the eligibility criteria for these benefits. By completing Form 8867, preparers demonstrate they have taken reasonable steps to verify the information provided by the taxpayer and are not knowingly claiming credits or filing statuses for which the taxpayer is ineligible. This aligns with Circular 230's emphasis on competence and integrity in tax practice.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Form 8867 is specifically tied to EIC, CTC/ACTC/ODC, AOTC, and Head of Household filing status. If a question mentions these, think Form 8867 and due diligence.
questions.memoryAid: ECHO: EIC, Child Tax Credit, Head of Household, Opportunity Credit. These are the credits/status that trigger Form 8867.

questions.question: A CP2000 notice from the IRS indicates:
options: The taxpayer has been selected for a field audit
The income, payment, or credit information the IRS has on file does not match what was reported on the return
The taxpayer qualifies for a refund
questions.explanation: A CP2000 notice is an automated underreporter notice sent when the information reported by third parties (W-2, 1099) does not match what the taxpayer reported on their return. It is not an audit but rather an inquiry under the Automated Underreporter (AUR) program. The notice proposes adjustments and gives the taxpayer 30 days to respond — they may agree, partially agree, or disagree with the proposed changes.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A CP2000 notice is generated by the Automated Underreporter (AUR) program and is not related to field audits. Field audits are conducted by IRS agents and involve a more in-depth examination of a taxpayer's records.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A CP2000 notice is issued when the income, payments, or credits reported to the IRS by third parties (e.g., employers, banks) do not match the amounts reported on the taxpayer's return. This discrepancy triggers the notice, prompting the taxpayer to reconcile the differences.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A CP2000 notice indicates a discrepancy, not confirmation of successful filing. Successful filing simply means the IRS received the return, not that the information is accurate.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A CP2000 notice usually indicates a potential underpayment of tax, not a refund. While resolving the discrepancy could *potentially* lead to a refund in some rare circumstances, the primary purpose of the notice is to address a mismatch that suggests additional tax is owed.

questions.educational: The Automated Underreporter (AUR) program is a key IRS compliance tool. It matches information returns (W-2s, 1099s, etc.) with taxpayer returns to identify potential underreporting of income. Understanding how the AUR program works and the notices it generates is crucial for representing clients effectively before the IRS and ensuring accurate tax compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that CP2000 notices require a timely response, usually within 30 days, and you should advise your client to gather all relevant documentation to support their position, whether agreeing, partially agreeing, or disagreeing with the IRS's proposed adjustments.
questions.memoryAid: CP2000: 'Compare Pay' - Reminds you that the notice is about comparing income reported by payers (employers, banks) with what the taxpayer reported.

questions.question: A taxpayer who needs to correct a previously filed individual income tax return should file:
options: Form 1040-X (Amended U.S. Individual Income Tax Return)
questions.explanation: Form 1040-X is used to correct errors on a previously filed Form 1040, 1040-SR, or 1040-NR. The amended return can be e-filed or mailed. It must show the original amounts, net changes, and corrected amounts. The taxpayer should explain the reason for the changes. An amended return claiming a refund must generally be filed within 3 years from the date the original return was filed or 2 years from the date the tax was paid.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing a new Form 1040 does not correct a previously filed return; it would be treated as a duplicate or a late filing, potentially leading to confusion and processing delays with the IRS. The IRS needs a specific mechanism to understand that you are amending a prior return, not filing a new one.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 1040-X, Amended U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, is specifically designed for taxpayers to correct errors or make changes to a previously filed Form 1040, 1040-SR, or 1040-NR. It allows taxpayers to explain the changes being made and provides a structured way for the IRS to process the amended information.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 843, Claim for Refund and Request for Abatement, is used to request a refund or abatement of certain taxes, penalties, or interest when no other form is available. It is not the appropriate form to correct errors on a previously filed individual income tax return. Form 843 is typically used for situations not directly related to income tax calculation errors.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 4868, Application for Automatic Extension of Time To File U.S. Individual Income Tax Return, is used to request an extension of time to file the original income tax return, not to correct a return that has already been filed. It has no bearing on amending a previously filed return.

questions.educational: Amended returns are crucial for maintaining tax compliance and accuracy. They allow taxpayers to correct errors, claim missed deductions or credits, and avoid potential penalties or interest. Understanding the statute of limitations for filing amended returns (generally three years from filing the original return or two years from paying the tax, whichever is later) is essential for effective tax planning and representation.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS prioritizes accuracy and transparency. When preparing Form 1040-X, provide a clear and concise explanation of the changes being made to avoid delays or further scrutiny from the IRS. Always retain copies of both the original and amended returns.
questions.memoryAid: 1040-X: 'X' marks the spot where you're making a correction.

questions.question: IRS Letter 3172 (Notice of Federal Tax Lien Filing and Your Right to a Hearing Under IRC 6320) gives the taxpayer:
options: 10 days to pay the full amount
30 days to request a Collection Due Process hearing
90 days to file a Tax Court petition
questions.explanation: Letter 3172 notifies the taxpayer that a Notice of Federal Tax Lien has been filed and informs them of their right to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing under IRC §6320. The taxpayer has 30 days from the date of the letter to request a CDP hearing using Form 12153. At the hearing, the taxpayer may challenge the lien filing, propose collection alternatives, or raise the underlying liability (if no prior opportunity existed).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS Letter 3172 does not provide a 10-day payment deadline. While prompt payment is always encouraged, the letter focuses on the taxpayer's right to a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing, not an immediate payment demand.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRS Letter 3172 explicitly informs the taxpayer of their right to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing under IRC §6320. The taxpayer has 30 days from the date of the letter to make this request using Form 12153. This hearing allows the taxpayer to challenge the lien, propose alternatives, or raise the underlying tax liability (if previously unaddressed).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 90-day period to file a Tax Court petition generally applies to deficiency notices (e.g., CP3219N), not notices of federal tax lien filings (Letter 3172). The CDP hearing process is separate from the Tax Court petition process.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While taxpayers have appeal rights within the IRS, the 60-day timeframe is not directly associated with the Letter 3172. The CDP hearing process outlined in the letter provides a specific avenue for addressing the lien filing, distinct from a general appeal process.

questions.educational: The Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing is a crucial taxpayer right, ensuring fairness and transparency in IRS collection actions. It stems from IRC §6320 and §6330, providing taxpayers an opportunity to challenge liens and levies before they become irreversible. Understanding CDP rights is essential for Enrolled Agents, as you must advocate for your clients' rights and explore all available options to resolve tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific IRS notice being referenced in the question. Each notice triggers different taxpayer rights and deadlines. Know the key deadlines for responding to common IRS notices, especially those related to collections.
questions.memoryAid: CDP = 30 Days (Collection Due Process hearing request deadline)

questions.question: All paid tax return preparers are required to obtain and use a:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6109(a)(4) and Treasury regulations, all paid tax return preparers must obtain and use a Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN) issued by the IRS. The PTIN must be included on every return or claim for refund the preparer signs. PTINs must be renewed annually. A preparer who fails to include a PTIN on a return may be subject to a $50 penalty per failure under IRC §6695(c).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a CPA license allows an individual to prepare tax returns, it is not a *universal* requirement for *all* paid tax return preparers. Many individuals without a CPA license can legally prepare returns for compensation, provided they meet other IRS requirements, such as obtaining a PTIN.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6109(a)(4) and its corresponding Treasury Regulations mandate that all paid tax return preparers must obtain and use a Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN). This PTIN must be included on any tax return or claim for refund they prepare for compensation. Failure to include a PTIN can result in penalties under IRC §6695(c).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While Enrolled Agents (EAs) are authorized to prepare tax returns, simply possessing an Enrolled Agent number is not the *only* requirement. All paid preparers, including EAs, must also have and use a PTIN. An EA number alone is insufficient for compliance.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A state business license may be required to operate a tax preparation business in certain states, but it is not a federal requirement applicable to *all* paid tax return preparers. The IRS requires a PTIN regardless of state licensing requirements.

questions.educational: The PTIN system was implemented to improve oversight and accountability within the tax preparation industry. By requiring all paid preparers to register and obtain a PTIN, the IRS can better track preparers, enforce regulations, and identify those who may be engaging in fraudulent or unethical practices. This directly relates to Circular 230, which governs the ethical conduct of tax professionals.
questions.examTip: Remember that the PTIN is a *federal* requirement for *all* paid tax return preparers. Don't confuse it with state-specific licensing or credentials like CPA or Enrolled Agent status, which have different requirements and scopes of practice.
questions.memoryAid: PTIN = Paid Tax Identification Number. If you're paid, you need a PTIN.

questions.question: The IRS may issue a summons under IRC §7602 to:
options: Arrest a taxpayer who refuses to pay
Examine books, records, papers, and other data, and to compel testimony under oath
Seize a taxpayer's property without notice
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7602, the IRS may issue a summons to examine books, records, papers, and other data relevant to determining the correctness of a return or tax liability. The summons may also compel testimony of the summoned person. If the summoned person does not comply, the IRS may seek judicial enforcement through the U.S. District Court. A summons is an administrative tool — it does not authorize arrest or seizure.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS summons power under IRC §7602 does *not* authorize arrest. Arrests for tax-related offenses require a separate criminal investigation and legal process, not a simple summons.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7602 explicitly grants the IRS the authority to issue summonses to examine books, records, papers, and other data relevant to ascertaining the correctness of any return, determining the liability of any person for any internal revenue tax, or inquiring into any offense connected with the administration or enforcement of the internal revenue laws. The summons can also compel testimony under oath from the summoned individual.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A summons is an administrative tool used to gather information. It does *not* authorize the IRS to seize a taxpayer's property. Seizure of property requires a separate legal process, such as a levy, and typically involves prior notice to the taxpayer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS summons power is an administrative tool for gathering information and does not grant the IRS the authority to impose criminal penalties. Criminal penalties can only be imposed after a trial in a court of law.

questions.educational: The IRS summons power under IRC §7602 is a critical tool for tax administration, allowing the IRS to investigate potential noncompliance. Understanding the scope and limitations of this power is essential for Enrolled Agents, as you must advise clients on their rights and obligations when faced with an IRS summons. The IRS must have a legitimate purpose for issuing the summons, and the information sought must be relevant to that purpose.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that a summons is an *administrative* tool for information gathering, not a substitute for legal proceedings like arrest, seizure, or criminal penalties. If a question mentions a summons, focus on the IRS's ability to request information and compel testimony.
questions.memoryAid: Summons = Seek, Understand, Muster, Monitor, Obtain, Notice, Enforce, Subpoena

questions.question: After the IRS assesses a tax liability, the taxpayer receives:
options: A phone call from the revenue officer
A notice and demand for payment (typically a CP14 notice for initial billing)
A summons to appear in court
No notification — the taxpayer must check their account online
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6303, after assessment, the IRS must send a notice and demand for payment to the taxpayer within 60 days. For individual income tax, the initial billing notice is typically a CP14 (Balance Due notice). The notice identifies the tax period, amount owed, and the deadline for payment. If the taxpayer fails to pay, additional notices follow (CP501, CP503, CP504) before the IRS takes enforcement action.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a revenue officer *might* eventually contact a taxpayer regarding an unpaid tax liability, this occurs much later in the collection process, typically after multiple notices have been issued and ignored. The initial notification is a written notice, not a phone call.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6303 mandates that the IRS issue a notice and demand for payment within 60 days of assessing a tax liability. This notice, often a CP14 for individual income tax, informs the taxpayer of the amount due, the tax period, and the payment deadline, initiating the collection process.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A summons to appear in court is a more serious action taken by the IRS, usually reserved for situations involving investigations, uncooperative taxpayers, or attempts to collect significant tax debts. It is not the initial notification after an assessment.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS is legally obligated to notify taxpayers of assessed tax liabilities. IRC §6303 requires the IRS to send a notice and demand for payment. Taxpayers are not solely responsible for proactively checking their accounts online to discover tax debts.

questions.educational: The notice and demand requirement ensures taxpayers are informed of their tax liabilities and have an opportunity to comply before the IRS takes more aggressive collection actions like liens or levies. This aligns with the IRS's duty to fairly administer tax laws and provide taxpayers with due process. Understanding the sequence of IRS notices is crucial for advising clients on how to respond to IRS actions and protect their rights.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the IRS collection process starts with a notice and demand (like a CP14), followed by escalating notices if payment isn't received. Knowing the order of these notices can help you advise clients on the appropriate response at each stage.
questions.memoryAid: NAD: Notice And Demand. The IRS *Notices* you, then *Asks* for money, then *Demands* it.

questions.question: Tax return preparers must retain a copy or list of all returns prepared, along with the taxpayer's name and identification number, for:
options: 3 years after the close of the return period
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1, a tax return preparer must retain a completed copy of each return or claim for refund, or a list containing the taxpayer's name, identification number, tax year, and type of return, for 3 years after the close of the return period. These records must be available for IRS inspection upon request.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While some tax-related documents have a 1-year retention requirement, this is not the standard retention period for tax returns prepared by a preparer. Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1 specifically requires a longer retention period for prepared returns.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1 mandates that tax return preparers retain a completed copy of each return or claim for refund, or a list containing the taxpayer's name, identification number, tax year, and type of return, for 3 years after the close of the return period. This ensures the IRS can review the preparer's work if necessary.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 5-year retention period is not the standard required by Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1 for tax returns prepared by a preparer. While the IRS generally has 3 years to assess additional tax, the preparer's retention requirement is specifically tied to the return period, not the assessment period.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A 7-year retention period is not the standard required by Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1 for tax returns prepared by a preparer. While the statute of limitations for certain tax-related actions can extend to 6 years in cases of substantial understatement of income, this does not dictate the return retention requirement for preparers.

questions.educational: The requirement for tax return preparers to retain copies of returns or lists of clients for 3 years after the close of the return period is a key component of IRS oversight and due diligence. This allows the IRS to examine a preparer's practices and ensure compliance with tax laws, promoting accuracy and ethical conduct within the profession. Understanding retention requirements is crucial for maintaining compliance and avoiding penalties as an Enrolled Agent.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific question being asked. Distinguish between the retention requirements for taxpayers versus tax preparers, and remember the 3-year rule for preparer return retention under Treas. Reg. §1.6107-1.
questions.memoryAid: Remember '3 for PREParer' – 3 years for PREParer return retention.

questions.question: A taxpayer may agree to extend the statute of limitations on assessment by signing:
options: Form 872 (Consent to Extend the Time to Assess Tax)
questions.explanation: Form 872 is used when both the taxpayer and the IRS agree to extend the time for assessing tax. This is commonly done when an examination is still in progress and the normal 3-year statute is about to expire. Form 872 extends the statute for a fixed period, while Form 872-A provides an open-ended (indefinite) extension. Signing is voluntary — the taxpayer is not required to agree, but refusal may result in the IRS issuing a statutory notice of deficiency based on available information.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 870, Waiver of Restrictions on Assessment and Collection of Deficiency in Tax and Acceptance of Overassessment, is used by a taxpayer to waive their right to contest an IRS assessment and agree to pay the deficiency. It does *not* extend the statute of limitations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 872, Consent to Extend the Time to Assess Tax, is the correct form used by both the IRS and the taxpayer to mutually agree to extend the period in which the IRS can assess additional tax. This is often used when an audit is ongoing and nearing the statute of limitations deadline.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative, authorizes an individual to represent a taxpayer before the IRS. It does *not* extend the statute of limitations for assessment.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 656, Offer in Compromise, is used by a taxpayer to propose a settlement of their tax debt for less than the full amount owed. It does *not* extend the statute of limitations for assessment, although submitting one may temporarily suspend collection activity.

questions.educational: The statute of limitations on assessment is generally three years from the date the return was filed (or its due date, if later). Extending this period via Form 872 allows the IRS more time to complete an audit or investigation without having to rush to issue a Notice of Deficiency. Understanding statutes of limitations is crucial for protecting your clients' rights and ensuring proper tax compliance, a key aspect of Circular 230 duties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific actions each form authorizes. Memorizing the form numbers and their purposes will save you time and reduce errors when answering procedural questions.
questions.memoryAid: 872: 'Extend the Time, Too!' (rhymes to help remember it extends the assessment time)

questions.question: Under IRC §6662, "negligence" is defined as any failure to:
options: Make a reasonable attempt to comply with the Internal Revenue Code, including failure to keep adequate books and records or to substantiate items properly
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6662(c), negligence includes any failure to make a reasonable attempt to comply with the provisions of the Internal Revenue Code. This includes failure to exercise reasonable care in preparing the return, failure to keep adequate records, and failure to substantiate items properly. "Disregard" includes any careless, reckless, or intentional disregard of rules or regulations. The negligence penalty is 20% of the underpayment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While failing to pay taxes on time can result in penalties, it falls under failure to pay penalties (IRC §6651), not the definition of negligence under IRC §6662.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6662(c) explicitly defines negligence as any failure to make a reasonable attempt to comply with the Internal Revenue Code. This includes failing to keep adequate books and records or to properly substantiate items, demonstrating a lack of reasonable care.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the IRS encourages electronic filing, failing to file electronically is not, in itself, considered negligence under IRC §6662. Penalties may apply in certain situations for failure to file electronically, but that is a separate issue from negligence.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While hiring a tax professional can help ensure compliance, not doing so is not automatically considered negligence under IRC §6662. Taxpayers are ultimately responsible for the accuracy of their returns, regardless of whether they use a professional.

questions.educational: The negligence penalty under IRC §6662 aims to encourage taxpayers to exercise reasonable care when preparing their tax returns. It's not about perfection, but about making a good-faith effort to understand and follow the tax laws. Understanding negligence penalties is crucial for EAs as you advise clients on tax compliance and potential penalty exposure.
questions.examTip: Remember that the negligence penalty under IRC §6662 is assessed on the underpayment of tax, so be prepared to calculate the penalty amount (20%) on exam questions involving underpayments due to negligence.
questions.memoryAid: Negligence = Not Enough Good Effort (NEGE)

questions.question: An individual owing $10,000 or less (not including penalties and interest) who has filed all required returns and has not had an installment agreement in the past 5 years qualifies for a:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6159(c), the IRS must accept a guaranteed installment agreement for individual taxpayers who: (1) owe $10,000 or less (excluding penalties and interest), (2) have filed all required returns for the past 5 years, (3) have not had an installment agreement during the past 5 years, and (4) agree to pay the liability within 3 years and comply with all tax laws during the agreement. The IRS cannot refuse this agreement if all conditions are met.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA) is considered when a taxpayer can't fully pay their tax liability within the statute of limitations. The facts in the question describe a situation where the taxpayer *can* pay within 3 years, making a PPIA inappropriate.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A Guaranteed Installment Agreement under IRC §6159(c) *must* be granted by the IRS if the taxpayer meets specific criteria: $10,000 or less owed (excluding penalties and interest), all returns filed for the past 5 years, no prior installment agreement in the past 5 years, and an agreement to pay within 3 years. The question explicitly states all these conditions are met.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a Streamlined Installment Agreement also exists for taxpayers owing $50,000 or less, the *Guaranteed* Installment Agreement has stricter requirements and *must* be granted if those requirements are met. The question's facts fit the Guaranteed Installment Agreement criteria, making it the more precise and correct answer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is granted when a taxpayer demonstrates an inability to pay *any* amount due to financial hardship. The question describes a taxpayer who can pay their liability within 3 years, making CNC status inapplicable.

questions.educational: Installment agreements are a critical tool for taxpayers struggling to meet their tax obligations. The IRS offers various types, each with specific eligibility requirements and payment terms. Understanding the nuances of each agreement, including the Guaranteed Installment Agreement's mandatory acceptance provision, is essential for providing effective representation to clients facing collection issues. This falls under the broader topic of collection alternatives, a key area on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific dollar thresholds and eligibility criteria for each type of installment agreement. Knowing the difference between a Streamlined and Guaranteed Installment Agreement is crucial, as the IRS *must* grant the latter if all conditions are met.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'G-FAST': Guaranteed, Filed, Amount ($10k), Statute (3 years), Tax laws (comply).

questions.question: A CP504 notice from the IRS is significant because:
options: It is a routine reminder to file a return
It is a final notice before levy, informing the taxpayer of the IRS's intent to levy and seize state tax refunds or other property
It confirms the taxpayer's return was accepted
questions.explanation: The CP504 notice is the final balance-due notice before the IRS takes enforcement action. It represents the IRS's intent to levy (seize) the taxpayer's state tax refund or other property. The notice series typically progresses: CP14 (initial billing) → CP501 (reminder) → CP503 (urgent reminder) → CP504 (intent to levy). After CP504, the IRS may issue Letter 1058 (Final Notice of Intent to Levy) or LT11, triggering CDP hearing rights.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A CP504 notice is not a routine reminder to file a return. It is issued only when a balance is due and previous notices have been sent, indicating a more serious situation than a simple filing reminder.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A CP504 notice is a critical communication from the IRS. It signifies the IRS's intent to levy, meaning they plan to seize assets like state tax refunds or other property to satisfy outstanding tax debt. This notice is a precursor to more aggressive collection actions, making it a final warning before enforcement.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A CP504 notice does not request additional documentation. It is a balance due notice, indicating the IRS believes the taxpayer owes money based on the information they already have.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A CP504 notice is the opposite of confirming a return was accepted without issue. It indicates a discrepancy or unpaid balance after the return has been processed.

questions.educational: The IRS uses a series of notices to inform taxpayers of issues with their tax accounts. Understanding the progression of these notices, from initial billing to intent to levy, is crucial for an Enrolled Agent. This knowledge allows you to proactively advise clients, negotiate payment options, and protect their assets from seizure, which is a core competency tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the CP504 is a 'final notice' before levy, and triggering a levy can have severe consequences for your client, so immediate action is required.
questions.memoryAid: CP504: 'C'ollection 'P'roceeding '504' sure to levy!

questions.question: A paid tax return preparer must sign the return and include:
options: Their name, PTIN, firm name and address, and EIN of the firm (if applicable)
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6695(b) and Treas. Reg. §1.6695-1, a paid preparer must sign every return they prepare for compensation and include: their name, their PTIN, the firm's name, the firm's address, and the firm's EIN (if applicable). Failure to sign a return results in a $50 penalty per failure (up to $27,000 per year). The signing preparer is the individual with primary responsibility for the return's overall substantive accuracy.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a paid preparer must include their name, this option is incomplete. IRC §6695(b) and Treas. Reg. §1.6695-1 require additional information such as the PTIN and firm details.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6695(b) and Treas. Reg. §1.6695-1 explicitly state that a paid tax return preparer must sign the return and include their name, Preparer Tax Identification Number (PTIN), the firm's name and address, and the Employer Identification Number (EIN) of the firm, if applicable. This ensures accountability and traceability for the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Including a Social Security number on a tax return prepared for compensation would be a violation of privacy and is not required. The PTIN serves as the unique identifier for paid preparers.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the firm's name is required, this option is incomplete. The paid preparer must also include their own name and PTIN, as well as the firm's address and EIN (if applicable), according to IRC §6695(b) and Treas. Reg. §1.6695-1.

questions.educational: The requirement for paid preparers to include specific identifying information on tax returns is crucial for IRS oversight and enforcement. It allows the IRS to track preparers, assess penalties for non-compliance, and ensure the accuracy and integrity of tax filings. This requirement is directly tied to Circular 230, which governs the ethical conduct of tax professionals, and the IRS's overall mission to collect taxes fairly and efficiently.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS emphasizes preparer accountability. If a question involves preparer responsibilities, always consider the requirements outlined in IRC §6695 and Circular 230, especially regarding signing returns and due diligence.
questions.memoryAid: Remember 'N-P-F-A-E': Name, PTIN, Firm Name, Address, EIN (if applicable) - the elements a paid preparer must include when signing a return.

questions.question: A superseding return differs from an amended return in that:
options: A superseding return is filed after the extended due date
A superseding return replaces the original return if filed before the due date (including extensions) and is treated as the original return
A superseding return is filed in a different tax year
There is no difference between a superseding return and an amended return
questions.explanation: A superseding return is a return filed after the original return but before the due date (including extensions). It completely replaces the original return and is treated by the IRS as if it were the original return. An amended return (Form 1040-X) is filed after the due date has passed. The distinction matters because a superseding return can change elections and carries the same status as an original return.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A superseding return must be filed *before* the due date, including extensions, not after. Filing after the extended due date would constitute an amended return.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A superseding return is indeed filed before the original due date (including extensions) and is treated as the original return. This is crucial because it allows taxpayers to correct errors or make different elections before the filing deadline without needing to amend.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The tax year is not the determining factor. Both superseding and amended returns relate to the same tax year as the original return being corrected.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is a significant difference. A superseding return replaces the original, while an amended return is a correction to the original after the filing deadline.

questions.educational: The distinction between a superseding return and an amended return is critical for tax planning and compliance. A superseding return allows taxpayers to make changes as if they were part of the original filing, including elections. Amended returns, filed after the deadline, are used to correct errors or omissions but do not have the same legal standing as the original or a superseding return, particularly regarding elections.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates provided in scenarios. If a return is filed before the due date (including extensions), consider whether it's a superseding return and what implications that has for elections and penalties.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Super' as 'Superior' - it comes first and replaces the original, while 'Amended' comes 'After' the deadline.

questions.question: The IRS Discriminant Inventory Function (DIF) score is used to:
options: Identify returns with the highest potential for audit adjustments based on statistical analysis
questions.explanation: The DIF score is a mathematically computed score assigned to each return based on statistical formulas. Returns with higher DIF scores have a greater potential for tax change upon examination. The IRS uses the DIF score as one factor in selecting returns for audit. The specific formulas are confidential and not disclosed to the public. Returns may also be selected for audit through informant tips, document matching, or related examinations.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The DIF score is an internal IRS tool for identifying potentially non-compliant returns, not for calculating refund amounts. Refund calculations are based on the information reported on the tax return itself and applicable tax laws.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Discriminant Inventory Function (DIF) is a system used by the IRS to score tax returns based on statistical analysis. Returns with higher DIF scores are flagged as having a higher probability of containing errors or inconsistencies that would result in a tax adjustment upon audit. This helps the IRS prioritize its audit resources.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Filing status is determined by the taxpayer based on their marital status and family situation as of the last day of the tax year. The DIF score has no bearing on determining a taxpayer's filing status.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The DIF score is used *after* a return is filed, not during the e-filing process. E-filed returns are processed electronically, and the DIF score is applied later to identify returns for potential audit.

questions.educational: The IRS uses the DIF score to efficiently allocate its audit resources. By statistically analyzing returns and identifying those with the highest potential for tax changes, the IRS can focus its efforts on areas where noncompliance is most likely. This is crucial for maintaining tax compliance and ensuring fair tax administration, a key responsibility highlighted in Circular 230 regarding an EA's duty to advise clients on potential penalties.
questions.examTip: Remember that the DIF score is an *internal* IRS tool. As an Enrolled Agent, you won't have access to a client's DIF score, but understanding its purpose helps you anticipate potential audit triggers based on common errors or omissions.
questions.memoryAid: DIF = Detect Inconsistencies Fast

questions.question: An individual taxpayer with prior-year AGI of $200,000 must pay estimated taxes equal to at least what percentage of the prior year's tax to avoid the estimated tax penalty using the prior-year safe harbor?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6654(d)(1)(C), if the taxpayer's prior-year AGI exceeds $150,000 ($75,000 for married filing separately), the safe harbor for estimated tax based on the prior year's tax is 110% of that year's tax liability. For taxpayers with AGI of $150,000 or less, the safe harbor is 100% of the prior year's tax. Since $200,000 exceeds $150,000, the taxpayer must pay at least 110% of the prior year's tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 90% is not the correct percentage for the prior-year safe harbor when the taxpayer's prior-year AGI exceeds $150,000. The safe harbor requires a higher percentage to avoid the estimated tax penalty.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 100% of the prior year's tax liability is the safe harbor amount only if the taxpayer's prior-year AGI does not exceed $150,000 ($75,000 if married filing separately). Since the taxpayer's AGI is $200,000, this option is incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §6654(d)(1)(C) states that if a taxpayer's prior-year adjusted gross income (AGI) exceeds $150,000 ($75,000 for married filing separately), they must pay at least 110% of the prior year's tax liability to meet the prior-year safe harbor and avoid estimated tax penalties. Because the taxpayer's AGI is $200,000, 110% is the correct percentage.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While paying 120% of the prior year's tax liability would certainly avoid the estimated tax penalty, it is not the minimum required amount under the prior-year safe harbor rule for taxpayers with AGI exceeding $150,000. The minimum required is 110%.

questions.educational: The estimated tax penalty exists to ensure that taxpayers pay their income tax liability throughout the year, rather than in a lump sum at the end of the tax year. The safe harbor rules, including the prior-year tax exception, provide taxpayers with a way to avoid the penalty by paying a specified percentage of their prior-year tax liability, offering a simpler calculation than estimating their current-year income.
questions.examTip: Remember the $150,000 AGI threshold for the 110% prior-year safe harbor rule; misremembering this threshold is a common mistake on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'High AGI, Higher Percentage': High AGI (over $150k) needs a higher percentage (110%) of last year's tax to be safe.

questions.question: An Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) issued by the IRS is:
options: A 6-digit number assigned to eligible taxpayers to help prevent the misuse of their SSN on fraudulent tax returns
A password for IRS online services only
questions.explanation: An IP PIN is a 6-digit number assigned by the IRS to help prevent identity theft on federal tax returns. When a taxpayer has an IP PIN, their return must include it to be accepted. A new IP PIN is issued each year. All taxpayers who can verify their identity are eligible to opt into the IP PIN program through their IRS online account. Previously, it was only available to confirmed victims of identity theft.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - An Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) is a separate, unique identifier and is not the taxpayer's Social Security number (SSN). The IP PIN is designed to protect the SSN from misuse.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) is a 6-digit number assigned annually by the IRS to eligible taxpayers. Its primary purpose is to prevent the fraudulent use of their Social Security number (SSN) on tax returns, ensuring only the legitimate taxpayer can file using that SSN.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A CAF (Centralized Authorization File) number is used by the IRS to identify a representative authorized to act on behalf of a taxpayer. It is unrelated to the Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) program, which focuses on identity theft prevention.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While an IP PIN is used when e-filing a tax return, it is not solely a password for IRS online services. It's a specific identifier required for filing the tax return itself, whether electronically or on paper.

questions.educational: The Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) program is a critical component of the IRS's efforts to combat identity theft, a significant threat to taxpayers and the tax system. By requiring the IP PIN on tax returns, the IRS can verify the filer's identity and prevent fraudulent refunds from being issued. This aligns with Circular 230's emphasis on due diligence and protecting the integrity of the tax system.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that IP PINs are not permanent and are issued annually. Be prepared to advise clients on how to obtain and use their IP PINs, especially if they are victims of identity theft.
questions.memoryAid: IP PIN = Identity Protection, Prevents Impersonation, Number

questions.question: If the IRS rejects an Offer in Compromise, the taxpayer may:
options: Only pay the full amount owed
Appeal the rejection to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals within 30 days of the rejection letter
File a petition in Tax Court immediately
Submit the same offer again without changes
questions.explanation: If the IRS rejects an OIC, the taxpayer has the right to appeal the rejection to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals within 30 days of the date of the rejection letter. The appeal is made by completing the request on the rejection letter or by submitting a written request. At the appeal, the taxpayer may provide additional information to support the offer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, the taxpayer may submit a new offer with different terms.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While paying the full amount owed is always an option, it's not the *only* option after an OIC rejection. The taxpayer has appeal rights and the possibility of submitting a revised offer.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Taxpayers have the right to appeal an OIC rejection to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals. This appeal must be made within 30 days of the date of the rejection letter, as outlined in IRS procedures for Offer in Compromise cases. This allows for an independent review of the initial decision.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Filing a petition in Tax Court immediately after an OIC rejection is incorrect. Tax Court jurisdiction generally arises from disputes over tax deficiencies, not OIC rejections. The taxpayer must first exhaust their administrative remedies within the IRS, such as the appeals process.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Submitting the *same* offer again without changes after a rejection is unlikely to be successful. The IRS has already determined the initial offer was unacceptable. A new offer should address the reasons for the original rejection and include different terms or additional information.

questions.educational: The Offer in Compromise (OIC) program allows eligible taxpayers to resolve their tax liabilities for a lower amount than what they owe. If the IRS rejects an OIC, the taxpayer has the right to appeal this decision to the IRS Independent Office of Appeals. This process ensures fairness and provides an opportunity for an independent review of the case, aligning with the principles of taxpayer rights and due process.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember the 30-day deadline for appealing an OIC rejection. Missing this deadline can significantly limit your client's options for resolving their tax debt.
questions.memoryAid: OIC Rejection? Appeal in 30!

questions.question: When the IRS corrects a math error on a tax return under IRC §6213(b), the taxpayer:
options: Has no right to dispute the correction
Has 60 days from the date of the notice to request that the IRS abate the assessment
Must file a Tax Court petition within 90 days
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6213(b), the IRS may summarily assess tax resulting from mathematical or clerical errors without issuing a Statutory Notice of Deficiency. However, the taxpayer has the right to request abatement of the assessment within 60 days of the math error notice. If abatement is requested, the IRS must either accept the request or issue a formal Statutory Notice of Deficiency, giving the taxpayer full appeal rights.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Taxpayers *do* have the right to dispute math error corrections. IRC §6213(b)(2) specifically grants taxpayers the right to request abatement of the assessment.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6213(b)(2) states that a taxpayer has 60 days from the date the IRS issues notice of a math error correction to request an abatement of the assessment. This allows the taxpayer to challenge the IRS's determination without immediately resorting to more formal procedures.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A Tax Court petition is generally filed in response to a Statutory Notice of Deficiency. Because a math error correction under IRC §6213(b) does *not* constitute a Statutory Notice of Deficiency, the 90-day period for filing a Tax Court petition does not apply *unless* the IRS denies the abatement request and *then* issues a Statutory Notice of Deficiency.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A math error correction does not automatically result in a refund. It may result in an increased tax liability, a decreased refund, or no change at all, depending on the nature of the error.

questions.educational: The IRS can quickly correct obvious math errors to efficiently process returns. However, taxpayers retain the right to challenge these corrections because even seemingly simple math can have complex tax implications. This balance between efficiency and taxpayer rights is a recurring theme in IRS procedures, especially regarding assessment and collection.
questions.examTip: Remember that math error notices under IRC §6213(b) do *not* trigger the same appeal rights as a Statutory Notice of Deficiency. The 60-day abatement request is the first step in disputing a math error correction.
questions.memoryAid: 6213(b) - 'B'e quick to abate in 60 days!

questions.question: An enrolled agent who discovers that another tax preparer has committed misconduct may report it to the:
questions.explanation: The IRS Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) has exclusive responsibility for practitioner discipline under Circular 230 (31 CFR Part 10). If an enrolled agent becomes aware of potential misconduct by another practitioner (such as incompetence, disreputable conduct, or violations of Circular 230), they may report it to OPR using Form 14157 (Complaint: Return Preparer). OPR investigates and may impose sanctions including censure, suspension, or disbarment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The local police department handles criminal matters. While some tax preparer misconduct could potentially involve criminal activity, the primary avenue for reporting ethical violations and Circular 230 infractions is through the IRS.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) is specifically tasked with overseeing and enforcing ethical standards and regulations for tax practitioners, including enrolled agents, as outlined in Circular 230. Reporting potential misconduct to OPR allows them to investigate and take appropriate disciplinary action.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - State Boards of Education oversee educational institutions and teacher certifications, not tax preparers. They have no jurisdiction over the ethical conduct or professional standards of tax practitioners.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) primarily focuses on consumer protection and preventing unfair business practices. While some tax preparer misconduct might fall under the FTC's purview if it involves deceptive advertising or fraud against consumers, the IRS OPR is the primary regulatory body for tax practitioner ethics and compliance with Circular 230.

questions.educational: Circular 230 establishes the regulations governing practice before the IRS, including ethical standards and due diligence requirements for tax professionals. The Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) enforces these regulations to maintain the integrity of the tax system and protect taxpayers from incompetent or unethical practitioners. Understanding Circular 230 and OPR's role is crucial for enrolled agents to uphold their professional responsibilities and avoid potential disciplinary actions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Circular 230 governs the conduct of tax professionals before the IRS, and the OPR is the enforcement arm. If a question involves ethical violations or misconduct by a tax preparer, OPR is likely the correct answer.
questions.memoryAid: OPR = Oversees Practitioner Responsibility

questions.question: Under IRC §6695(a), a paid preparer who fails to furnish a copy of the return to the taxpayer is subject to a penalty of:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6695(a), a tax return preparer must furnish a completed copy of the return or claim for refund to the taxpayer no later than the time the return is presented for the taxpayer's signature. Failure to provide a copy results in a $50 penalty per return, with a maximum annual penalty of $27,000. The copy must include all schedules and attachments.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While some preparer penalties exist in the $25 range, the penalty for failure to furnish a copy of the return to the taxpayer is specifically $50 per return under IRC §6695(a).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6695(a) explicitly states that a tax return preparer who fails to furnish a copy of the return to the taxpayer at the time the return is presented for signature is subject to a penalty of $50 per return. This requirement ensures taxpayers have a record of what was filed on their behalf.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A $100 penalty is associated with other preparer penalties, such as understating a taxpayer's liability due to an unreasonable position (IRC §6694(a)), not the failure to provide a copy of the return to the taxpayer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A $250 penalty is not directly associated with the failure to furnish a copy of the return to the taxpayer under IRC §6695(a). This amount is more closely aligned with penalties related to aiding and abetting the understatement of tax liability under IRC §6701, which is a more serious offense.

questions.educational: The requirement for paid preparers to furnish a copy of the return to the taxpayer serves as a crucial safeguard for taxpayers, ensuring transparency and accountability in the tax preparation process. This requirement helps taxpayers understand their tax obligations and provides them with a record for future reference, aligning with the broader ethical responsibilities outlined in Circular 230 regarding due diligence and competence.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific dollar amounts for common preparer penalties. Focus on memorizing the penalties associated with IRC §6695 as they are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Furnish Fifty' - Furnish a copy, Fifty dollar penalty.

questions.question: Under IRC §7623(b), a whistleblower who provides information leading to the detection of tax underpayments exceeding $2,000,000 may receive an award of:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7623(b), for cases involving more than $2,000,000 in dispute, the IRS Whistleblower Office must award 15% to 30% of the collected proceeds (including penalties, interest, additions to tax, and additional amounts). The award may be reduced if the whistleblower planned and initiated the underpayment. For smaller cases under IRC §7623(a), the IRS may pay up to 15% at its discretion.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §7623(b) specifies that when the tax underpayments exceed $2,000,000, the award is 15% to 30% of the collected proceeds, not 5% to 10%. This lower percentage range is not applicable in cases meeting the $2,000,000 threshold.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7623(b) mandates that the IRS Whistleblower Office award 15% to 30% of the collected proceeds (including penalties, interest, additions to tax, and additional amounts) to a whistleblower when the information provided leads to the detection of tax underpayments exceeding $2,000,000. This range reflects the incentive Congress intended to provide for reporting significant tax evasion.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A flat $50,000 award is not specified under IRC §7623(b) for whistleblowers providing information leading to the detection of tax underpayments exceeding $2,000,000. The award is calculated as a percentage of the collected proceeds, not a fixed amount.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a 50% award might seem appealing, IRC §7623(b) caps the whistleblower award at 30% of the collected proceeds for cases involving tax underpayments exceeding $2,000,000. A 50% award is not authorized by the statute.

questions.educational: IRC §7623 incentivizes individuals to report tax law violations by offering a percentage of the recovered proceeds. The higher percentage range for substantial underpayments ($2,000,000+) reflects the significant impact these cases have on tax revenue and the complexity often involved in uncovering them. Understanding whistleblower provisions is crucial for EAs as it relates to taxpayer rights and IRS enforcement mechanisms.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dollar thresholds in whistleblower questions. Remember that the 15%-30% range applies specifically to cases exceeding $2,000,000 in underpayments.
questions.memoryAid: Think '2 Million, 2 Numbers': $2,000,000 triggers the 15%-30% award range for whistleblowers.

questions.question: When the IRS levies on a taxpayer's wages or salary, the levy:
options: Is continuous and attaches to all future wage payments until the levy is released
Requires a new notice for each pay period
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6331(e), a levy on wages, salary, and other income is continuous from the date the levy is served until the levy is released. This means the employer must continue to pay the levied portion of each paycheck to the IRS until the IRS formally releases the levy. In contrast, a levy on a bank account is a one-time levy that attaches only to funds in the account on the date the levy is served.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A wage levy is continuous, not a one-time event. IRC §6331(e) specifically states that a levy on salary or wages is continuous from the date of service until the levy is released.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6331(e) dictates that a levy on wages is continuous. The employer is legally obligated to withhold and remit the specified amount from each paycheck until the IRS releases the levy. This ensures consistent collection of the debt.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS does not need to issue a new levy notice for each pay period when levying wages. The initial levy notice remains in effect until the debt is satisfied or the levy is released.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A wage levy applies to all wages, not just overtime pay. The levy is calculated based on the taxpayer's total gross wages, taking into account exemptions for dependents and the standard deduction, as determined by Form 668-W.
whyWrong.4: Why option E is WRONG - There is no option E.

questions.educational: The continuous wage levy, as defined in IRC §6331(e), is a critical tool for the IRS to collect outstanding tax debts. Unlike a one-time levy on a bank account, a wage levy ensures a steady stream of payments. Understanding the rules surrounding levies is essential for EAs, as they frequently advise clients facing collection actions and negotiate payment alternatives like installment agreements or offers in compromise.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the type of levy described in the question. Remember that wage levies are continuous, while bank levies are generally one-time events. Knowing the difference is key to answering collection-related questions correctly.
questions.memoryAid: Wage Levy = 'Withholding Always Going Every payday' (WAGE)

questions.question: When the IRS levies on a taxpayer's bank account, the bank must hold the funds for how many days before sending them to the IRS?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6332(c), when the IRS serves a levy on a bank, the bank must hold the funds in the account (as of the date of the levy) for 21 days before remitting them to the IRS. This 21-day holding period gives the taxpayer time to resolve the matter — by contacting the IRS, setting up a payment arrangement, or demonstrating that the levy creates a hardship. After 21 days, the bank sends the funds to the IRS.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Banks are legally required to hold levied funds for a specific period before remitting them to the IRS, so immediate transfer is not permitted under IRC §6332(c).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6332(c) mandates that banks hold funds subject to an IRS levy for 21 days. This holding period allows the taxpayer time to address the levy with the IRS, explore payment options, or demonstrate financial hardship.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While 30 days might seem like a reasonable timeframe, the IRS specifically requires banks to hold levied funds for 21 days, as outlined in IRC §6332(c).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The holding period for levied funds is shorter than 60 days. IRC §6332(c) clearly states that banks must hold the funds for 21 days before sending them to the IRS.

questions.educational: The 21-day holding period for bank levies is a crucial taxpayer protection built into the IRS collection process. It provides a window for taxpayers to negotiate with the IRS, potentially avoiding asset seizure and allowing for alternative payment arrangements. Understanding these procedural safeguards is essential for representing clients effectively before the IRS.
questions.examTip: Remember the 21-day rule for bank levies! On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific timeframes associated with IRS collection actions, as these are frequently tested.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '21' as 'Two Weeks and Change' – a little over two weeks for the bank to hold the levy.

questions.question: A third party whose property has been wrongfully levied by the IRS may file a civil action under IRC §7426 within:
options: 9 months from the date of the levy
2 years from the date of the levy
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7426(a), a third party (not the taxpayer) whose property has been wrongfully levied may bring a civil action against the United States in federal district court. The action must be filed within 9 months from the date of the levy. Alternatively, the third party may file an administrative claim with the IRS before the 9-month period expires. If the administrative claim is filed, the third party has an additional 12 months from the date of the IRS decision to file suit.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §7426 specifies a 9-month period, not 30 days, for a third party to file a civil action for wrongful levy.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The statute of limitations for a wrongful levy action under IRC §7426 is 9 months from the date of the levy, not 90 days.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §7426(a)(1) explicitly states that a third party has 9 months from the date of the levy to bring a civil action against the United States for wrongful levy. This timeframe ensures a balance between allowing the third party sufficient time to discover and address the wrongful levy and providing the government with a reasonable period to resolve the matter.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While 2 years is a statute of limitations in tax law for certain actions, it does not apply to wrongful levy actions by third parties. IRC §7426(a)(1) clearly specifies a 9-month period, potentially extended by an administrative claim.

questions.educational: IRC §7426 provides a remedy for individuals or entities whose property has been wrongfully seized by the IRS to satisfy another taxpayer's liability. This provision is crucial for protecting the rights of innocent parties and ensuring fairness in tax collection procedures. Understanding the statute of limitations and administrative claim process is essential for representing clients in levy situations.
questions.examTip: Remember the 9-month rule for wrongful levy actions under IRC §7426, and be aware of the potential 12-month extension if an administrative claim is filed. This is a frequently tested area on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '9 Months to Right the Wrongful Levy' to remember the timeframe for filing a civil action under IRC §7426.

questions.question: Under IRC §7433, a taxpayer may sue the IRS for damages if an IRS employee:
options: Recklessly or intentionally disregards any provision of the Internal Revenue Code or regulations in connection with collection of tax
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7433, a taxpayer may bring a civil action for damages against the United States if any officer or employee of the IRS recklessly or intentionally disregards any provision of the Code or any regulation in connection with the collection of federal tax. The taxpayer may recover actual economic damages plus costs of the action. The maximum award is $1,000,000 ($100,000 for negligence). The taxpayer must first exhaust administrative remedies.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Sending a routine billing notice is a standard IRS procedure and does not constitute reckless or intentional disregard of the Internal Revenue Code or regulations. IRC §7433 is concerned with egregious actions, not routine administrative tasks.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7433 specifically allows a taxpayer to sue the IRS when an employee recklessly or intentionally disregards a provision of the Internal Revenue Code or regulations *in connection with the collection of federal tax*. This provision aims to protect taxpayers from abusive or unlawful collection practices by the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A standard correspondence audit is a normal IRS procedure used to verify information on a tax return. While taxpayers may disagree with the outcome of an audit, it does not inherently involve reckless or intentional disregard of the law that would trigger IRC §7433.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The denial of a penalty abatement request, in itself, does not constitute reckless or intentional disregard of the Internal Revenue Code or regulations. The IRS has discretion in granting or denying penalty abatements, and a denial, even if perceived as unfair, is not grounds for a lawsuit under IRC §7433 unless it stems from a clear violation of law.

questions.educational: IRC §7433 provides a limited remedy for taxpayers harmed by egregious IRS misconduct during tax collection. This section balances the IRS's need to enforce tax laws with the taxpayer's right to fair treatment. Understanding taxpayer rights and IRS responsibilities is crucial for Enrolled Agents, especially regarding collection procedures and potential remedies for clients.
questions.examTip: Remember that IRC §7433 lawsuits are limited to situations involving reckless or intentional disregard of the law during *collection* activities. Mere disagreements with IRS interpretations or audit findings are insufficient grounds for a suit.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '7433' as 'Sue the IRS for Bad Behavior During Collection' – the last three digits of the section number resemble a sideways 'B' for 'Bad'.

questions.question: The tax practitioner-client privilege under IRC §7525 applies to:
options: Tax advice communications between a taxpayer and a federally authorized practitioner (EA, CPA, attorney) in noncriminal tax matters before the IRS and in noncriminal federal court proceedings
All communications between a taxpayer and any paid preparer
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7525, communications between a taxpayer and a federally authorized tax practitioner (enrolled agent, CPA, or attorney) are privileged to the same extent as attorney-client privilege, but ONLY for tax advice in noncriminal tax matters before the IRS or in noncriminal tax proceedings in federal court. The privilege does NOT apply to: (1) criminal matters, (2) state tax proceedings, (3) tax return preparation (as opposed to tax advice), (4) written communications involving tax shelters.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The tax practitioner-client privilege under IRC §7525 specifically excludes tax return preparation. While advice *related* to tax return positions can be privileged, the act of preparation itself is not.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7525 extends attorney-client privilege to federally authorized tax practitioners (EAs, CPAs, and attorneys) for tax advice. This privilege is limited to noncriminal tax matters before the IRS and in noncriminal federal court proceedings, mirroring the scope of attorney-client privilege in those contexts.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The tax practitioner-client privilege only applies to federally authorized tax practitioners (EAs, CPAs, and attorneys). It does not extend to all paid preparers, such as unlicensed individuals or those working under the supervision of a qualified professional.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The tax practitioner-client privilege under IRC §7525 explicitly excludes criminal tax matters. Attorney-client privilege still applies in criminal matters, but the extended privilege for EAs and CPAs does not.

questions.educational: The tax practitioner-client privilege aims to level the playing field by allowing taxpayers to seek advice from qualified professionals without fear of disclosure, similar to the protection afforded by attorney-client privilege. This encourages open communication and accurate tax compliance. Understanding the scope and limitations of this privilege is crucial for EAs to advise clients effectively and ethically, especially when navigating complex tax issues or potential disputes with the IRS.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between tax advice and tax return preparation when evaluating privilege questions; preparation is generally *not* privileged, while advice *is*, provided the other requirements of IRC §7525 are met.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'ACT No Crims': Advice, CPA/EA/Attorney, Tax (before IRS/Federal Court), No Criminal.

questions.question: A taxpayer may elect small tax case (S case) procedures in the U.S. Tax Court if the amount in dispute is:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7463, a taxpayer may elect small tax case (S case) procedures in Tax Court if the amount of the deficiency or claimed overpayment (including penalties) does not exceed $50,000 for each tax year. S cases offer simplified procedures, are less formal, and taxpayers may represent themselves. However, S case decisions cannot be appealed by either party and do not serve as precedent for other cases.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While $25,000 might seem like a reasonable limit for simplified procedures, the actual threshold for electing small tax case procedures in Tax Court is higher. Choosing this option indicates a misunderstanding of the specific dollar limits defined in IRC §7463.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §7463 explicitly states that a taxpayer can elect small tax case procedures in the U.S. Tax Court if the amount of the deficiency or claimed overpayment (including penalties) does not exceed $50,000 for each tax year. This allows for a more streamlined and accessible process for resolving smaller tax disputes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $75,000 exceeds the statutory limit of $50,000 for electing small tax case procedures in the U.S. Tax Court. Selecting this option demonstrates a lack of precise knowledge of the relevant IRC section and its limitations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The maximum amount for a small tax case election is $50,000. $100,000 is significantly higher than this limit, making this option incorrect according to IRC §7463.

questions.educational: Small tax case procedures exist to provide a more accessible and less formal avenue for taxpayers to resolve disputes with the IRS involving smaller amounts. This is particularly beneficial for taxpayers who may not be able to afford legal representation. Electing S case status means the decision is final and cannot be appealed, reflecting a trade-off between simplicity and the right to further legal recourse.
questions.examTip: Remember the $50,000 limit for small tax cases, as it's a frequently tested detail. Also, be aware that while S cases are simpler, the decisions are final and non-appealable, a key consideration for your clients.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'S' for 'Small' and 'Fifty' for $50,000 to remember the limit for Small Tax Cases.

questions.question: Under IRC §6323(j), the IRS may withdraw a filed Notice of Federal Tax Lien if:
options: The taxpayer pays 50% of the liability

Filing was premature or not in accordance with IRS procedures, the taxpayer has entered into an installment agreement (unless the agreement provides otherwise), withdrawal will facilitate collection, or it is in the best interest of the taxpayer and the government
The taxpayer files a new return
The lien has been filed for more than 1 year
questions.explanation: Under IRC §6323(j), the IRS may withdraw a filed NFTL if: (1) filing was premature or not in accordance with administrative procedures, (2) the taxpayer entered into an installment agreement that does not provide for a lien filing, (3) withdrawal will facilitate collection, or (4) withdrawal is in the best interest of both the taxpayer and the government. Withdrawal removes the public notice but does not eliminate the underlying lien.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Paying 50% of the liability is not, on its own, grounds for the IRS to withdraw a Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL) under IRC §6323(j). While partial payment might be a factor considered in determining if withdrawal would facilitate collection or be in the best interest of the taxpayer and the government, it's not a standalone justification.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6323(j) specifically outlines circumstances under which the IRS *may* withdraw a filed NFTL.
